hierarchy.lvl2: Generating a full species parameter set for PnET-Succession using shade/drought/waterlogging tolerance, temperature information, and evergreen/deciduous status

hierarchy.lvl1: Notebook 3 - Getting the initial parameters values
hierarchy.lvl2: Generating a full species parameter set for PnET-Succession using shade/drought/waterlogging tolerance, temperature information, and evergreen/deciduous status

records.content:
It might be that you find yourself without initial values for a given species that you want to calibrate; maybe because this tree species has never been calibrated/parameterized in PnET-Succession of in the PnET models at large, or maybe there are a lot of parameters for which you don’t have initial values.

The following code cell is made to generate a full species parameter set for several species for which you have a full Latin name. This is done by using the calibration tips of Eric Gustafson in the 

user guide of PnET-Succession v5.1. In it, Eric proposes initial parameters values dependant on wherever your species in a deciduous or evergreen tree, and its shade tolerance. It’s far from ideal; but seeing that Eric is one of the most knowledgeable experts on the model, I think it’s a good start.

The drought and temperature tolerance parameters will be derived using the same methodology as the previous code sections. For the rest of the parameters, the rules with which they will be derived can be found in the 

user guide of PnET-Succession v5.1. The evergreen/deciduous type and the shade tolerance for the species is derived from the dataset of 

Niinemets and Valladares 2006. If your tree species cannot be found in the dataset, then the function will not work; you will have to provide your own shade tolerance and evergreen/deciduous classification. The shade tolerance must range from 1 (very intolerant to shade) to 5 (very tolerant).

To use the code cell, simply provide the code and full Latin name of the species for which you want to generate a full species parameter set at the beginning of the code.

If you indicate species for which you have already indicated parameters above, the values of their parameters will be replaced !

Simply make sure that the species you indicate here don’t have parameters already indicated in the tables of the previous sections, or the following code cell will simply replace these values.

This code cell only generate PnET-Succession species parameters; you might still need to provide core species parameters !

The core species parameters of LANDIS-II (longevity, effective dispersal distance, vegetative reproduction probability, etc.) are not dealt with by the following code cell. This is because these parameter (which correspond to life history traits of the species) are more difficult to find, and you cannot predict them using the simple rules proposed by Eric Gustafson.

To facilitate our calibration of tree species in Canada, I have compiled published values of these parameters for as many canadian species as possible and tried to find these values using the sources often used in LANDIS-II studies for these species. The code will therefore retrieve these and add them to the .json file containing the initial core species parameters values.

If one of the species you want to calibrate isn’t found in the parameters I have compiled, you will get an error message saying that you need to find these parameters by yourself. See the section about parameter sources above for some potential sources to help you find them. If you have no source, you will surely require expert opinion. Once you have obtained them, indicate these core species parameters in the corresponding table in the previous sections. You don’t need to add these species to the PnET-Succession species parameter table that comes afterward, as these parameters will be obtained by the following code cell.

SLWmax is derived from the published data of 

Diaz et al. 2022

With a bit of trial and error, I’ve noticed that the SLWmax values predicted from the propositions of Eric Gustafson in the 

user guide of PnET-Succession v5.1 were not always very good, especially for some peculiar species like the Tamarack (Larix laricina).

Therefore, I’ve downloaded and used the data of 

Diaz et al. 2022 which contains values of LMA (Leaf Mass Area), which is the same thing as the Specific Leaf Weight (SLW). I’ve taken the dataset and removed all non-tree species from it, and removed columns that were not useful to my use here.

However, what we need is SLWmax, while the measure in the 

Diaz et al. 2022 dataset is most likely the average SLW of leaves for a species.

To derive SLWmax from the LMA of 

Diaz et al. 2022 (that I will now call SLW_{avg}), I therefore used the following equation that is based on the parameter SLWDel and the IMAX generic parameter :

SLWmax = \frac{SLW_{avg}}{1 - \left( SLWDel \times \frac{IMAX - 1}{2} \right)}

This formula accounts for the fact that PnET-Succession distributes foliage into IMAX with even proportions, and reduces the specific leaf weight linearly from the top sublayer (SLWmax) to the bottom sublayer based on the SLWDel proportion. For example, if SLWDel=0.2 (the standard value for deciduous species), the SLW of each subsequent sublayer decreases by 20% of the SLWmax value. This creates an arithmetic progression:

Sublayer 1: SLWmax×1.0

Sublayer 2: SLWmax×(1−SLWDel)

Sublayer 3: SLWmax×(1−2×SLWDel)

...and so on for IMAX layers.

Therefore, using this formula means that the average of SLW accross the layers will be equal to the value found in 

Diaz et al. 2022 for the species for the given SLWmax we will find.

Not that while the dataset contains a measure of nitrogen in the leaves (Nmass), I haven’t used it to derive the FolN parameter. This is because in the 

user guide of PnET-Succession v5.1, Eric Gustafson says that it is more important to make sure that FolN is ranked relative to the shade tolerance of the species simulated, rather than using empirical values. Therefore, I believe that using the FolN scale that changes with the shade tolerance of a species as Gustafson proposes is a better way of doing things here.

from functionsForCalibration import *
import json
import pandas as pd
import os


# Load dictionnary of additional species codes/full latin names
# INDICATE THE NAME OF THE SPECIES FOR WHICH YOU WANT AN ADDITIONAL PARAMETER SET HERE !
# BE CERTAIN TO INDICATE THEIR FULL LATIN NAME CORRECTLY : It is used for a lookup in different datasets.
additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary = {
    "ACER.SAH":"Acer saccharum",
    "LARI.LAR":"Larix laricina",
    "POPU.TRE":"Populus tremuloides"
}

niinemetsValladaresData = pd.read_csv("./ReferencesAndData/Others/NiinemetsEtValladares_AppendixTable_full.csv")

dendonckerEtAlData = pd.read_csv("./ReferencesAndData/Others/DendonckerEtAl2026_Average_niche_all_spp.csv")

resultDict = {}

# For each species,
for speciesName in additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary:
    print(f"Getting parameters for {speciesName}")
    resultDict[speciesName] = dict()
    
    # Get shade tolerance/Drought tolerance and tree/wood type from niinemets and al.
    # If the species is not found in Niinemets and Valladares, we ask for a manual input.
    if additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName] not in list(niinemetsValladaresData["Species"]):
        print("Species " + str(additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]) + " is not found in the data of Niinemets and Valladares (2006). Please enter the information of the species manually :")
        shade_tolerance = -999
        while shade_tolerance < 1 or shade_tolerance > 5:
            shade_tolerance = float(input("Please input the shade tolerance of " + str(additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]) + " (from 1 = very intolerant to 5 = very tolerant"))
        tree_type = "none"
        while tree_type != "evergreen" and tree_type != "deciduous":
            tree_type = input("Please input the tree type (evergreen or deciduous) of " + str(additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]) + " :")
        wood_type = "none"
        while wood_type != "hardwood" and tree_type != "softwood":
            wood_type = input("Please input the wood type type (hardwood or softwood) of " + str(additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]) + " :")
    # If the species is in Niinemets and Valladares, we get the values from the dataset.
    else:
        rowWithSpecies = niinemetsValladaresData[niinemetsValladaresData["Species"] == additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]]
        # Some values in the dataset of Niinemets and Valladares are inferior to 1 or superior to 5;
        # not sure why. Not explained in the article. We deal with these situations by cropping the values to 1-5.
        shade_tolerance = min(5 , max(1, list(rowWithSpecies["Shade tolerance"])[0]))
        if list(rowWithSpecies["Evergreen"])[0] == "Y":
            tree_type = "evergreen"
        else:
            tree_type = "deciduous"
        if list(rowWithSpecies["Gymnosperm"])[0] == "Y":
            wood_type = "softwood"
        else:
            wood_type = "hardwood"

    # This function uses the rules indicated by Eric Gustafson in the user guide of PnET-Succession v5.1.
    # There is two parameters from which Gustafson has no rule to get them : TOFol and FrActWd.
    # For these, I used the averaged values for deciduous/evergreen and hardwood/softwoods in previous publications of PnET-Succession parameters
    # this gives the following :
    # - TOFol : 1 for deciduous trees, 0.3 for evergreens
    # - FrActWd : 0.000035 for hardwoods, 0.000029 for softwoods

    # We retrieve the parameter IMAX, which is needed for computing SLWmax in the following function
    IMAX = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/InitialGenericParameters.json'))["IMAX"]
    resultDict[speciesName].update(get_parameters_gustafson_rules(shade_tolerance,
                                                                  tree_type,
                                                                  wood_type,
                                                                 additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName],
                                                                 IMAX))


    ############################################################
    # Get the values of H1/H2/H3/H4
    # Getting drought and wl tolerance from NiinemetsAndValladares
    # Again, if the species is not in the niinemets and valladares dataset, we ask for the data
    if additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName] not in list(niinemetsValladaresData["Species"]):
        print("Species " + str(additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]) + " is not found in the data of Niinemets and Valladares (2006). Please enter the information of the species manually :")
        drought_tolerance = -999
        while drought_tolerance < 1 or drought_tolerance > 5:
            drought_tolerance = float(input("Please input the drought tolerance of " + str(additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]) + " (from 1 = very intolerant to 5 = very tolerant"))
        waterlogging_tolerance = -999
        while waterlogging_tolerance < 1 or waterlogging_tolerance > 5:
            waterlogging_tolerance = float(input("Please input the waterlogging tolerance of " + str(additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]) + " (from 1 = very intolerant to 5 = very tolerant"))

    # If the species is in Niinemets and Valladares, we get the values from the dataset.
    else:
        rowWithSpecies = niinemetsValladaresData[niinemetsValladaresData["Species"] == additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]]
        drought_tolerance = min(5 , max(1, list(rowWithSpecies["Drought tolerance"])[0]))
        waterlogging_tolerance = min(5 , max(1, list(rowWithSpecies["Waterlogging tolerance"])[0]))
    
    resultDict[speciesName].update(predict_H_parameters(
    drought_tolerance=drought_tolerance,
    waterlogging_tolerance=waterlogging_tolerance,
    plot=False,
    gam_summary=False
    ))
    
    # Getting the values for the temperature parameters
    # if the species is not in the Dendoncker et al. dataset, we ask for the data
    if additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName] not in list(dendonckerEtAlData["Species"]):
        print("Species " + str(additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]) + " is not found in the data of Dendoncker et al. (2026). Please enter the information of the species manually :")
        MAT_q05 = -9999
        while MAT_q05 < -273:
            MAT_q05 = float(input("Please input MAT_q05 (5th percentile of the Mean Annual Temperature for the entire spatial distribution range of the species " + str(additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]) + ", i.e. the lowest annual temperatures in places where it survives) :"))
        
        MAT_q95 = -9999
        while MAT_q95 < -273:
            MAT_q95 = float(input("Please input MAT_q95 (95th percentile of the Mean Annual Temperature for the entire spatial distribution range of the species " + str(additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]) + ", i.e. the highest annual temperatures in places where it survives) :"))
        
        MTWM_q95 = -9999
        while MTWM_q95 < -273:
            MTWM_q95 = float(input("Please input MTWM_q95 (95th percentile of the Mean temperature of the Warmest Month for the entire spatial distribution range of the species " + str(additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]) + ", i.e. the highest mean temperature of the warmest month in places where it survives) :"))
        
    # If the species is in the dataset, we get the values from the dataset.
    else:
        rowWithSpecies = dendonckerEtAlData[dendonckerEtAlData["Species"] == additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]]
        MAT_q05 = list(rowWithSpecies["MAT.q05"])[0]
        MAT_q95 = list(rowWithSpecies["MAT.q95"])[0]
        MTWM_q95 = list(rowWithSpecies["MTWM.q95"])[0]
    
    resultDict[speciesName].update(predict_temperature_parameters_single_species(
                        species_code = speciesName,
                        species_latin_name = additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName],
                        MAT_q05 = MAT_q05,
                        MAT_q95 = MAT_q95,
                        MTWM_q95 = MTWM_q95,
                        return_df = False,
                        plotAndSummaries = False)[0][speciesName])

    
    print(resultDict[speciesName])
    print("\n")


# Export the new species to the existing .json files
dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json'))
# In case no initial PnET Species parameters exist
if os.path.exists('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json'):
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json'))
else:
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters = dict()
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["LandisData"] = "PnETSpeciesParameters"
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"] = dict()
dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/InitialGenericParameters.json'))

for species in resultDict:
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species] = dict()
    for parameter in resultDict[species]:
        dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species][parameter] = str(resultDict[species][parameter])

for species in dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"]:
    # We put the parameters in the same order for all species
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species] = {k: dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species][k] for k in sorted(dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species])}

with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/InitialGenericParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters, f)

# At the end : add the additional species codes/full latin names to the existing species dictionnary name
with open('./SpeciesParametersSets/speciesNamesDictionnary.json', 'r') as file: speciesDict = json.load(file)
speciesDict.update(additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/speciesNamesDictionnary.json", "w") as f: json.dump(speciesDict, f)

# We also edit the names in the core parameter file
coreSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json', 'r'))
for key in additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary.keys():
    # print(key)
    # print(speciesCodeDict[key])
    coreSpeciesParameters = replace_in_dict(coreSpeciesParameters, additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[key], key)
with open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json', 'w') as json_file:
    # Step 4: Write the dictionary to the file
    json.dump(coreSpeciesParameters, json_file)

Notebook 4 - Getting the targets and parameters needed from the next steps from empirical data and previous studies

records.content:
Here, I catalog the other data needed for the calibration process, in addition to the initial parameters (see 

here for the parameters) and to the climate and soil data used in the simulations for the calibration (see 

here for soil and climate data).

This other data will be used to :

Target values to match during the calibration (e.g. biomass growth curves)

Determine the value of some parameters directly (e.g. maximum longevity)

Establish ranges of parameter values in which we should remain during the calibration

Expected outcomes of simulations (e.g. competition outcomes, climate change outcomes, etc.)

I will write here as much suggestions a possible for future researchers that will want to calibrate for their own research or study area, but I will also indicate what I will be using in these notebooks.

hierarchy.lvl2: Getting Shade tolerance, drought tolerance and waterlogging tolerance from Niinemets and Valladares (2006)

records.content:
The article from 

Niinemets and Valladares 2006 is often used in LANDIS-II studies. The authors have painstakingly created scores ranging from 1 to 5 for shade, drought and waterlogging tolerance of many tree species in North America and in Europe.

We will use these scores to make statistical regressions to obtain some parameters easily for species for which parameters haven’t been published before (e.g. for parameter H3 and H4 for drought, etc.).

Since the species names in their table are the full Latin names (e.g. Abies balsamea) but I’ll be using the NFI in the files I’m producing (e.g. ABIE.BAL), we’ll need to do a little conversion.

import pandas as pd
import json

niinemetsValladaresData = pd.read_csv("./ReferencesAndData/Others/NiinemetsEtValladares_AppendixTable_full.csv")

with open('./SpeciesParametersSets/speciesNamesDictionnary.json', 'r') as file: speciesDict = json.load(file)

SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets = {}
for species in speciesDict.keys():

    SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[species] = dict()
    
    if speciesDict[species] not in list(niinemetsValladaresData["Species"]):
        print("Species " + str(speciesDict[species]) + " is not found in the data of Niinemets and Valladares (2006). Please enter the information of the species manually :")
        
        shade_tolerance = -999
        while shade_tolerance < 1 or shade_tolerance > 5:
            shade_tolerance = float(input("Please input the shade tolerance of " + str(speciesDict[species]) + " (from 1 = very intolerant to 5 = very tolerant"))
        
        drought_tolerance = -999
        while drought_tolerance < 1 or drought_tolerance > 5:
            drought_tolerance = float(input("Please input the drought tolerance of " + str(speciesDict[species]) + " (from 1 = very intolerant to 5 = very tolerant"))
        
        waterlogging_tolerance = -999
        while waterlogging_tolerance < 1 or waterlogging_tolerance > 5:
            waterlogging_tolerance = float(input("Please input the waterlogging tolerance of " + str(speciesDict[species]) + " (from 1 = very intolerant to 5 = very tolerant"))

        evergreen_status = "None"
        while evergreen_status != "Y" and evergreen_status != "N":
            evergreen_status = str(input("Is the species " + str(speciesDict[species]) + " evergreen (in contrast to deciduous) ? Y or N "))

        gymnosperm_status = "None"
        while gymnosperm_status != "Y" and gymnosperm_status != "N":
            gymnosperm_status = str(input("Is the species " + str(speciesDict[species]) + " a gymnosperm ? Y or N "))

        SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[species]["Shade"] = shade_tolerance
        SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[species]["Drought"] = drought_tolerance
        SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[species]["Waterlogging"] = waterlogging_tolerance
        SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[species]["Evergreen"] = evergreen_status
        SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[species]["Gymnosperm"] = gymnosperm_status
        
    else:
        rowWithSpecies = niinemetsValladaresData[niinemetsValladaresData["Species"] == speciesDict[species]]
        # We crop to 1-5 as some values bellow 1 and above 5 for some species
        SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[species]["Shade"] = max(1, min(list(rowWithSpecies["Shade tolerance"])[0], 5))
        SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[species]["Drought"] = max(1, min(list(rowWithSpecies["Drought tolerance"])[0], 5))
        SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[species]["Waterlogging"] = max(1, min(list(rowWithSpecies["Waterlogging tolerance"])[0], 5))
        SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[species]["Evergreen"] = str(list(rowWithSpecies["Evergreen"])[0])
        SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[species]["Gymnosperm"] = str(list(rowWithSpecies["Gymnosperm"])[0])

print(SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets)

with open('./ReferencesAndData/Functional Traits/SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets, f)

hierarchy.lvl2: Estimating Maximum LAI target from the methodology of Gustafson

hierarchy.lvl1: Notebook 4 - Getting the targets and parameters needed from the next steps from empirical data and previous studies
hierarchy.lvl2: Estimating Maximum LAI target from the methodology of Gustafson

records.content:
In the user guide of PnET-Succession, Eric Gustasfson proposes to deduce the value of the Halfsat parameter of a given tree species based on its shade tolerance, with the following equivalence :

Shade Tol Category

Tolerant

Somewhat-tol

Intermediate

Somewhat-intol

Intolerant

Maximum LAI

<5.0

<4.5

<3.5

<2.8

<2.5

We will use a simple regression model to predict the Maximum LAI target for each species based on these numbers in the following cell.

import numpy as np
import json

def get_max_lai(shade_tolerance: float) -> dict:
    """
    Returns the Maximum LAI target for a given shade tolerance score (1–5)
    using linear interpolation between known discrete data points.
    """
    scores = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], dtype=float)
    lai    = np.array([2.5, 2.8, 3.5, 4.5, 5.0], dtype=float)

    max_lai = np.interp(shade_tolerance, scores, lai)

    return {"Maximum LAI target": round(float(max_lai), 4)}


with open('./SpeciesParametersSets/speciesNamesDictionnary.json', 'r') as file: speciesDict = json.load(file)

with open('./ReferencesAndData/Functional Traits/SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets.json', 'r') as file: SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets = json.load(file)

maxLAIDict = {}

for species in speciesDict.keys():
    maxLAIDict[species] = get_max_lai(SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[species]["Shade"])
    SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[species].update(maxLAIDict[species])

print(SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets)

with open('./ReferencesAndData/Functional Traits/SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets, f)

hierarchy.lvl1: Notebook 4 - Getting the targets and parameters needed from the next steps from empirical data and previous studies
hierarchy.lvl2: Getting Longevity and Max Longevity (TYPMORT and MAXMORT from Loehle (1988))

records.content:
What we will call here “Longevity” and “Max Longevity” are necessary to calibrate the senescence part of the growth curve for our species with the methodology of Brian Sturtevant’s team (see notebook 6).

The difference between the two longevity is this, per the team of Brian Sturtevant who explained their methodology to me :

Longevity is the rule-of-thumb, expert opinion longevity for a given tree species. This is the age at which it’s expected that a tree of a given species will die, in a give region. These are the number often reported in the literature, and used in the core species parameter file of LANDIS-II as its “Longevity” parameter for each tree species used.

Max Longevity is the real, empirical maximum age that a tree species can reach in the study area. It’s often not reported and difficult to find. A good way to find it is to look at sample plot data and find the oldest trees ever found for a given species (which can be difficult to find as the “age” data is often not directly available in sample plots data).

Brian Sturtevant and his team have used these two notions to calibrate the senescence part of the growth curves in ideal conditions as explained in 

​.​/6​.Monoculture​_ideal​_conditions​.py.  This part from 

this supplementary material of Reese et al. (2025) describes Brian’s methodology of explains it all :

We used an unpublished review and analysis led by John Du Plissis at the University of Minnesota-Duluth to define both tree species longevity (the age at which a species age cohort can no longer persist) and the initiation of decline that is defined by the age-related photosynthesis reduction factor (PsnAgeRed) parameter in PnET Succession. In general, the maximum species longevity of Loehle (1988) corresponds with the tree species longevity parameter, while the typical species’ longevity from the same publication determines the initiation of decline, where PsnAgeRed is calibrated such that the fAGE (age reduction factor) begins to decline (~0.99) at the Typical Mortality age of the species.

The data used by Brian’s team for Typical Longevity and Max Longevity are cited as coming from 

Loehle (1988) in ReeseEtAl_CanJForRes_2024_supplA.docx ; it’s an article that seem to compute them based on several characteristics of tree species.

In the end, Brian’s team is using a combination of data from 

Loehle (1988) and estimates found by John Du Plissis (yet unpublished). I’m going to use the data from 

Loehle (1988), which should be enough for our goals until the data from Du Plissis is published.

Loehle (1988), defines “Typical” versus “Maximum” longevity in the following terms :

Typical longevity is the age of the older trees in an old-growth stand, whereas maximum longevity is the upper known maximum above which very few trees are found.

We can find the value of typical longevity and maximum longevity from the Appendix 1 of 

Loehle (1988) (see end of the .pdf). The following code cell will pull the values from the appendix, which I have compiled in the file ./ReferencesAndData/Others/Loehle1987_Appendix1_WithTolerancesFromNiinemetsandValladares.csv. The file contains the data of 

Loehle (1988), but with added columns from 

Niinemets and Valladares 2006 - shade/drought/waterlogging tolerance and evergreen/gymnosperm status -, and data from 

Dendoncker et al. 2026 for the temperatures in the range of species. I’ve used these additional columns to tree to see if I could build a statistical regression to predict the Longevity and Max Longevity for species that are not in Loehle’s study; but the resulting performance of the models was not very good. I think they could be improved with several variables linked to longevity - growth rate, volumetric heat content, age of sexual maturity -, but I don’t have the time for it now.

What if my species are not in Loehle’s study ?

You will have to obtain estimates of the longevity and maximum longevity, and indicate them at the end of the following code cell.

Here are some sources that might be useful for you to obtain estimates :

https://​extension​.illinois​.edu​/sites​/default​/files​/maximum​_tree​_age​_and​_longevity​.pdf

https://​pmc​.ncbi​.nlm​.nih​.gov​/articles​/PMC10011738​/​#sec3

https://​www​.cambridge​.org​/core​/journals​/radiocarbon​/article​/abs​/radiocarbon​-dating​-opens​-up​-new​-frontiers​-in​-the​-study​-of​-tree​-longevity​-insights​-from​-angiosperm​-trees​/D0B293603AB4AB049F67EB26400A8C0F

I’m hesitant to recommend for you to go and find the “oldest trees” in different databases that you might find to estimate the maximum longevity for the species. It seems like extremely old/ancient trees often exist for a given species, but they are really outliers. The numbers given by Loehle don’t seem to go as far as this, as the definition of the maximum mortality remains the upper known maximum above which very few trees are found.

You have to take into account that the maximum longevity will be used in the calibration step 1 (in ideal conditions) to define the age at which an age cohort will ultimatly die because of the fAge photosynthesis reduction factor of PnET-Succession, making the cohort die even without any disturbance or competition affecting it. But the truth is that in real life, trees most often die because of disturbances or competition or diseases; and you will never simulate all of them. As such, I deem it more realistic to not indicate very high age values for the maximum longevity (e.g. 500 years or more), and remain the values indicated by Loehle. This way, you will reproduce the fact that an overwhelming amount of trees do die of different little things even if they don’t suffer fire or insect outbreaks or others, but maybe because of a mushroom or a bacteria, etc.

# Make code to read Loehle's typ and max mort and put it in the dictionnary.

import pandas as pd
import json

loehleData = pd.read_csv("./ReferencesAndData/Others/Loehle1987_Appendix1_WithTolerancesFromNiinemetsandValladares.csv")

with open('./SpeciesParametersSets/speciesNamesDictionnary.json', 'r') as file: speciesDict = json.load(file)

with open('./ReferencesAndData/Functional Traits/SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets.json', 'r') as file: SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets = json.load(file)

for species in speciesDict.keys():
    
    if speciesDict[species] not in list(loehleData["Species"]):
        print("Species " + str(speciesDict[species]) + " is not found in the data of Loehle (2006). Please enter its longevity and maximum longevity by hand at the end of this code cell.")
        
        SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[species]["Longevity"] = "None"
        SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[species]["Maximum longevity"] = "None"
        
    else:
        rowWithSpecies = loehleData[loehleData["Species"] == speciesDict[species]]

        longevityOfSpecies = list(rowWithSpecies["TYPMORT (years)"])[0]
        maximumLongevityOfSpecies = list(rowWithSpecies["MAXMORT (years)"])[0]

        if pd.isna(longevityOfSpecies):
            print("Species " + str(speciesDict[species]) + " is in the data of Loehle (2006), but doesn't have a longevity value. Please enter its longevity by hand at the end of this code cell.")
            SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[species]["Longevity"] = "None"
        else:
            SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[species]["Longevity"] = longevityOfSpecies
        
        if pd.isna(maximumLongevityOfSpecies):
            print("Species " + str(speciesDict[species]) + " is in the data of Loehle (2006), but doesn't have a maximum longevity value. Please enter its maximum longevity by hand at the end of this code cell.")
            SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[species]["Maximum longevity"] = "None"
        else:
            SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[species]["Maximum longevity"] = maximumLongevityOfSpecies


########################################################################################
# SECTION TO MANUALLY INDICATE LONGEVITY AND MAXIMUM LONGEVITY FOR YOUR MISSING SPECIES
########################################################################################
# Simply uncomment the following lines and fill them with your values.
# Be certain to indicate the code you're using for your species, not their full latin name.
#
# SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets["SPECIES.CODE"]["Longevity"] = 100
# SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets["SPECIES.CODE"]["Maximum longevity"] = 200

print(SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets)

with open('./ReferencesAndData/Functional Traits/SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets, f)

hierarchy.lvl1: Notebook 4 - Getting the targets and parameters needed from the next steps from empirical data and previous studies
hierarchy.lvl2: Biomass peak height and peak timing from empirical biomass growth curves.

records.content:
This is an essential part of the calibration process, especially the first step in which we will calibrate each species in “ideal” conditions of growth (mild climate, good soil water retention, etc.). These “ideal” conditions are important to isolate the effect of other parameters and other sources of mortality, so that we can focus on the basic parameters of growth and senescence for your species (see 

the first calibration step).

Basically, what you want is data that allows you to infer a biomass growth curve for each given species, showing the evolution of the biomass of a cohort for the species in conditions that are as ideal as possible.

As of today, I’ve run across three different strategies to get this data. I’ll describe them in the following sections.

hierarchy.lvl1: Notebook 4 - Getting the targets and parameters needed from the next steps from empirical data and previous studies
hierarchy.lvl3: Option 1 : Sample plot data.
hierarchy.lvl2: Biomass peak height and peak timing from empirical biomass growth curves.

hierarchy.lvl1: Notebook 4 - Getting the targets and parameters needed from the next steps from empirical data and previous studies
hierarchy.lvl3: Option 1 : Sample plot data.
hierarchy.lvl2: Biomass peak height and peak timing from empirical biomass growth curves.

records.content:
By looking at individual tree data from sample plot data, you can infer tree biomass and tree age, making each tree measurement or plot measurement a point on your graph.

By looking at the points that are at the top of the cloud of points that will result, you can get and idea of the maximum biomass a tree can reach for a given age, which should give you good information about the growth curve. This is a method used by many research so far.

The problem is that sample plot data is often messy, full of outliers, biases, needing sharing agreements, etc. As such, it will require important work to clean, and several important assumptions will influence your results enormously. For example, the “tree age” data is often missing, and you’ll have to infer it from DBH; and you’ll also have to choose how to translate your individual tree biomass into biomass/surface (as all LANDIS-II biomass output is interpreted in biomass/surface, not biomass/tree).

This data can still be quite useful to derive certain characteristics of the growth curve (e.g. the age of the peak of growth for a given species).

In Canada, acquiring sample plot data has been very much facilitated by the publication of the 

MAGPlot database. It contains standardized and unified plot data across all Canadian provinces, with tree DBH as the best measure available at the plot level. Some of the data (e.g. plot coordinates) are randomized to preserve sensible information.

hierarchy.lvl1: Notebook 4 - Getting the targets and parameters needed from the next steps from empirical data and previous studies
hierarchy.lvl3: Option 2 : Tree growth models.
hierarchy.lvl2: Biomass peak height and peak timing from empirical biomass growth curves.

hierarchy.lvl1: Notebook 4 - Getting the targets and parameters needed from the next steps from empirical data and previous studies
hierarchy.lvl3: Option 2 : Tree growth models.
hierarchy.lvl2: Biomass peak height and peak timing from empirical biomass growth curves.

records.content:
These models are complex equations developed by foresters, industries or governmental agencies to try and predict growth or yield for different species in different conditions. These are often calibrated based on sample plot data; as such, the work of cleaning/dealing with sample plot data is often done for you. An example is the famous 

Forest Vegetation Simulator from the USDA. Other such models are simply growth curves published in the literature, sometimes in books.

The curves produced by these models are often in biomass/surface, which makes things easier to compare with LANDIS-II outputs than sample plot. You can also tweak the parameters of these models so that they predict biomass growth in conditions as ideal as possible, making it close to what you’re looking for.

The main issue is that these models can still produce pretty unrealistic curves; might not be available for your study area/region; and might still need parameters derived from sample plot data (e.g. Site index and maximum basal area for FVS). So while they can make your life simpler, they can still be difficult to use.

FVS can be compiled into a Dockerfile and use in Python; I’ve tried it at first and will keep the functions that I’ve written in a separate document for you to re-use if necessary.

Be warned that the decreasing part of the curves produced by these often often don’t include mortality by senescence, which can make the cruve growth forever, making it difficult to identify the biomass peak which is very important for calibration purposes.

Tree growth models have been used by several research teams, including Brian Sturtevant’s and Eric Gustafson (the latter being the author of the calibration tips for PnET-Succession that are the basis of these documents).

records.content:
Some forest inventories contain data that allow you to infer tree or stand age and the biomass of your species of interest. For example, the 

National Forest Inventory of Canada proposes raster maps of several forest attributes Canada-wide, including pixel/stand forest age, and the biomass of each individual species that are considered in the forest inventory.

If you use each pixel from the maps of a single year as a point and plot a cloud of points on a biomass/time axis, you might get a good estimation of a growth curve, with a growth peak and a initial growth (the two most important parts in our case). This is, in essence, a 

“space-for-time” substitution, where you look at many different points in space that will be forests at different stages of growth, rather than looking at the long-term history of a few forests.

The big limitation of this data is that you often get one “stand age” or “pixel age” value per pixel, rather than a separate age value for the trees of a given species in the pixel. As such, in a pixel of 80 years, you cannot know if the biomass of species X comes from trees that are 80 years old, or if these trees are younger. But you can interpret the upper points of the cloud of points as “the maximum biomass that a species can reach for this stand age”, which is pretty close to the mark of what you’re looking for.

Another big limitation is that in most pixels (if not all), your species will be mixed with other species. This is especially true if you are calibrating a “companion” species (the type of tree species that is never abundant, but yet found in many forests). You can restrict the points of your data to only the pixels where more than 50% of the biomass comes from your species of interest; but you might not get enough points for some rarer species. In these case, you will probably need to develop a correction of biomass to try and have an estimate of the biomass that your species can reach when it composes 100% of the stand. You might do this by making a statistical model that attempts to predict the biomass of your species in a stand or pixel based on the age of the pixel, and the % of composition of the species. You might want to train your statistic model on only the top percentile of points (as you’re looking out for the most biomass your species can achieve for a given age).

This data is often generated through a combination of forest inventory sampling (aerial photography, etc.) and sample plot data (which are assigned to stand or pixels via assignation algorithms like the kNN method). As such, it often contains information from sample plots, but which has already been cleaned and dealt with.

The biomass data is often expressed in biomass/surface, making it easy to compare to LANDIS-II outputs.

This is the data I will be using here. From what I heard, other teams have been using such data in the past.

What is the spatial extent for which I should take growth data for the calibration ? And what climate data should I use depending on the origin of my growth data ?

See 

the introductory notebook about some important notions before beginning the calibration for detailed answers.

What data should I use to calibrate variants, like southern variants that are more adapted to heat ? And what variant I am calibrating for if I use this methodology ?

See 

the introductory notebook about some important notions before beginning the calibration for detailed answers.

records.content:
After investigation, and since my own use case is to calibrate PnET-Succession for several landscapes in Canada, I’ve chosen to go with option 3 (Forest inventory data). More specifically, I’ m going to use the NFI (National Forest Inventory of Canada).

See :

https://​nfi​.nfis​.org​/en/ for general information and visualization of some of the maps

https://​open​.canada​.ca​/data​/en​/dataset​/ec9e2659​-1c29​-4ddb​-87a2​-6aced147a990 to download the maps

Beaudoin et al. (2018) for details about how the maps were generated.

Here, I’m using data from the most recent year (2011). I’m using the map /NFI_Data/NFI_MODIS250m_2011_kNN_Structure_Stand_Age_v1.tif for the stand (pixel) ages; /NFI_Data/NFI_MODIS250m_2011_kNN_Structure_Biomass_TotalLiveAboveGround_v1.tif for biomass of stands; and a series of rasters like /NFI_Data/NFI_MODIS250m_2011_kNN_Species_Abie_Bal_v1.tif that indicate the proportion of /NFI_Data/NFI_MODIS250m_2011_kNN_Structure_Biomass_TotalLiveAboveGround_v1.tif that corresponds to the species of interest (e.g. Abies balsamea in that case). All maps are currently in ./ReferencesAndData.

I’m also using several “mask” rasters to restrict or differentiate the pixel data from different provinces in Canada (e.g. quebecMask.tif, etc., all in ./ReferencesAndData).

records.content:
Here, before we use this dataset, I quickly show how it looks like by focusing on the highest points of the point clouds that it produces. Focusing on these highest points will give us an idea of the “best growth” that the species can achieve through by looking at different pixels from the same year throughout all of Canada.

# We read data from the NFI for our free species, and plot the distribution of the points above 95% of the maximum value in
# each 3 years window (which allows us to keep the top points)
from functionsForCalibration import *
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt


# We define the path to where the rasters are
species_path = "./ReferencesAndData/NFI_Data/NFI_MODIS250m_2011_kNN_Species_Abie_Bal_v1.tif"
age_path = "./ReferencesAndData/NFI_Data/NFI_MODIS250m_2011_kNN_Structure_Stand_Age_v1.tif"
biomass_path = "./ReferencesAndData/NFI_Data/NFI_MODIS250m_2011_kNN_Structure_Biomass_TotalLiveAboveGround_v1.tif"
# We define the paths to the mask rasters for each province
dictOfMasks = {"NS":"./ReferencesAndData/SpatialBoundaries/novaScotiaMask.tif",
              "QC":"./ReferencesAndData/SpatialBoundaries/quebecMask.tif",
              "ON":"./ReferencesAndData/SpatialBoundaries/ontarioMask.tif",
              "MT":"./ReferencesAndData/SpatialBoundaries/manitobaMask.tif",
              "SK":"./ReferencesAndData/SpatialBoundaries/saskatchewanMask.tif",
              "AB":"./ReferencesAndData/SpatialBoundaries/albertaMask.tif",
              "BC":"./ReferencesAndData/SpatialBoundaries/britishColumbiaMask.tif",}

plottingNFISpeciesUpperBiomassByProvince(species_path,
                                         age_path,
                                         biomass_path,
                                         "Abies balsamea",
                                         dictOfMasks)





As such, we can see that :

The NFI data shows some pretty clear growth patterns (initial growth, peak, senescence)

We see that the different provinces can have very different growth peaks and initial growth/senescence. Upon closer inspection (plotting again but only for points with at least 50% of biomass composed of Abies balsamea), it seems to mostly be due to the differences in the mixture/competition of the species in difference province. SK, AB, MT and BC don’t have many points where Abies balsamea is dominant. Meanwhile, NS, QC and ON have a lot of it.

records.content:
Based on the NFI data shown and explored in the previous section, we now have to produce estimated growth curves based on this data that we will be able to use as a “target” for the first step of the calibration of PnET-Succession, where we will try to match the curves produced by PnET-Succession with the data.

Since the first calibration step is based on growth curves in “ideal” conditions, where the species we are calibrating have no competition for water and for light, we especially have to estimate the growth curves for monospecific stands, where the relative abundance of the species is 100%. Unfortunately, pixels that contains only one species are very rare in the NFI rasters since the pixels are 250x250m, and because in nature, monospecific stands can be quite rare for many species. Even worse, some species have a very low amount of pixels in which they have a high relative abundance, or even none at all. A good example here is Acer rubrum, the red maple, which has a low amount of points with relative abundance above 50% when compared to the two other species we are calibrating here, Abies balsamea and Picea mariana. I expect that many other tree species available in the NFI will have the same issue.

As such, I propose to use statistical modelling to use the information contained in the pixels we do have (where the relative abundance of any given species is below 100%) in order to generate growth curves for different level of abundances, and especially for a 100% abundance. I set up to use Generalized Additive Models, as they model non-linear effects of variables quite well, and because I expect the effect of the factors that will be used in this model (forest age and relative species abundance) will be non-linear.

In this approach, we will treat every pixel from the NFI raster as a single data point associated with 3 variables, one for the different raster : age of the pixel; biomass of the species X in the pixel; and relative abundance (in %) of species X in the pixel. By grouping these points into age windows, we can then use a GAM to predict the upper percentile of biomass of species X observed for a given age window, and for a given % relative abundance. As such, by predicting the upper percentile per age window, the GAM will predict how much maximal biomass species X can achieve for a given age, and a given % relative abundance.

Once the GAM is fitted, we make predictions for a 100% relative abundance, which is what we want. These predictions will not be perfect, as very few (if none) pixels with a 100% relative abundance exist, as said before; and as such, the model will be extrapolating outside of the data on which it has been fitted. But whatever the approach we use (i.e. using the FVS model, or using sample plot data), the problem will be the same : as monospecific stands are rarely observed in nature, any estimate that we’re trying to get here will be based on an extrapolation one way or another. This is the limitation of using a first calibration step in “ideal conditions”, although using this approach has some great advantages. For more about this, take a look at 

0​.1​.Some​_important​_notions​.ipynb where this question is talked about in details.

Having these GAMs fitted will thus allow us to produce growth curves predicted by the GAM for different level of % relative abundance for the species X, including the 100% curve that we are looking for.

records.content:
I’ve made some first attempts fitting some relatively simple GAMs on the data of the NFI for the three species I’m calibrating here. Unfortunately, this didn’t go well : the growth curves produced by the GAM was quite non-sensical, and didn’t fit the points very well. I’m not an expert in statistics, but I expect that this was because the model being fitted on patterns within the data that I couldn’t see with my human eyes (as in my eyes, the growth pattern visible in the data was quite clear). Maybe it’s the overabundance of low-abundance pixels, and the presence of some high-abundance pixels with relatively low biomass values and other outliers tended to create unexpected and absurd GAM behaviour.

I solved this issue by preprocessing the data (see figure below) to 1) reduce the number of points, 2) remove the outliers, and 3) force a monotonic increasing rule regarding the effect of abundance of the high percentiles of biomass. This pre-processing is done by defining windows of age and % relative abundance; in each of these windows, we gather all of the pixels/points that are inside of it (i.e. pixels with an age and a % relative abundance for species X that have the value for the window); and then, we compute the upper percentile of biomass for the pixels/points in this window. We repeat the process across all age/% relative abundance windows possible (without superposing the windows).

Furthermore, the function can remove points that break the monotonic increasing tendancy of the effect of the abundance on the biomass; i.e. if a percentile for an abundance window of 80 is lower than the percentile of the previous abundance window (e.g. 75) for the same age window, we will not take it into account. This is done to clarify the tendancy of the effect of the abundance on the biomass to help the GAMs fit better on the data, using the assumption that points that do not follow this tendancy are deemed to be errors or extremes.

Here is an depiction of the procedure in visual form :

This pre-processing is highly customizable, allowing you to define the size of the windows, the percentile computed, or to exclude certain pixels; see the function createPercentilesBiomassDatasetsFromNFIData in functionsForCalibration.py . You can put the argument printPlot as True in order to see the resulting points datasets.

The function createPercentilesBiomassDatasetsFromNFIData will generate four points datasets that correspond to four different spatial extent where we take the data from the NFI : the study landscape we are calibrating for; the ecozones that directly intersect with the study landscapes; the same ecozones + the ones that surrounds them in Canada; and finally, all of the data from the NFI for all of Canada. This is done so that we can fit GAMs and produce estimated growth curves for these different spatial extents, in order to compare the curves we obtain. The goal will be to select a curve that seems as realistic as possible while trying to select data as close to the study landscape as possible, in order to avoid mixing a lot of intra-specific variability (different phenotypes, variants, etc.) from the species in our dataset. However, sometimes, the data from the study landscape will not be enough to produce realistic curves, because there is not enough data points for the species in it (especially points with enough % relative abundance for the species).

As such, the function getGamDiagnostics (used after createPercentilesBiomassDatasetsFromNFIData) will compute some diagnostic values for the 100% abundance curves produced by the GAMs fitted on each of these datasets, and then use a simple algorithm to determine the best curve, and export it to a .csv file where it can be used in the subsequent notebooks for the calibration. The selection is made by first determining which curves are considered “valid” according to three diagnostics : a pseudo R2 > 0.85; a curve shape that is truly concave; and a percentage of negative biomass values along the curve (for the entire age range of the curve) being inferior to 20%. Then, we simply select the first valid curve in the subset of points that is the smallest (i.e. closest to the study landscape), in order to reduce the intra-specific variability present in the subset of points on which our estimation is based.

records.content:
The most robust GAM structure I’ve found after many trials accross the data for the three species we’re calibrating here is the following. It’s a GAM structure that produces mostly realistic curves (e.g. curves that are concave, with clearly defined growth peaks, and start near 0) across all species and data subsets (e.g. study landscape, ecozone, surrounding ecozones, and Canada).

\text{High Biomass Percentile} = s(age, by(\% abundance), \text{with a concave constraint}) +  s(\% abundance, by(age), \text{with a concave constraint}) + s(age, \text{with a concave constraint}) + s(\% abundance, \text{with a concave constraint})

Here, the by statement indicates a simple interaction between the two factors (see 

the documentation of the pyGAM package). We can also use a more complex interaction using a tensor (te()), but all of my attempts using tensors resulted in curves that didn’t make sense. It seems that the tensor was too sensitive to missing data, and tended to dip quickly into a negative effect, leading to non-sensical curves.

records.content:
The following code cell takes some time and some RAM

These cells can take a bit of time to run and will a good amount of RAM on your computer, in order to load and process the large rasters of the NFI.

If the kernel crashes, it’s most likely because of a lack of RAM.

The following code cell might ask for your input

If, for one of your species, the GAMs cannot produce a satisfying predicted curve for 100% abundance based on the four criterias used to validate them (not too much negative values, etc.), then the code cell will ask for your input to choose the geographic area which gives the best curve according to you.

from functionsForCalibration import *
import json
import os

with open('./SpeciesParametersSets/speciesNamesDictionnary.json', 'r') as file: speciesDict = json.load(file)

with open('./ReferencesAndData/Functional Traits/SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets.json', 'r') as file: SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets = json.load(file)

for speciesCode in speciesDict:

    speciesName = speciesDict[speciesCode]

    print("#" * 60)
    print("PROCESSING NFI DATA FOR SPECIES : " + str(speciesName))
    print("#" * 60)
    print("\n\n")

    # Let's check if the abundance raster for the species exists; if not, we need to download it.
    # WARNING : To find the raster for the species, the species code here must be the ones used by the NFI.
    # see https://nfi.nfis.org/resources/general/3-TreeSpeciesList-Version4.5.pdf
    fileNameSpeciesAbundanceRaster = 'NFI_MODIS250m_2011_kNN_Species_' + str(speciesCode.split(".")[0][0:4].capitalize()) + '_'+ str(speciesCode.split(".")[1][0:3].capitalize()) + '_v1.tif'
    if not os.path.exists('./ReferencesAndData/NFI_Data/' + str(fileNameSpeciesAbundanceRaster)):
        print("Abundance raster not found for this species; downloading from NFI FTP.")
        pathToFile = 'https://ftp.maps.canada.ca/pub/nrcan_rncan/Forests_Foret/canada-forests-attributes_attributs-forests-canada/2011-attributes_attributs-2011/' + str(fileNameSpeciesAbundanceRaster) 
        import urllib.request; urllib.request.urlretrieve(pathToFile, './ReferencesAndData/NFI_Data/' + str(fileNameSpeciesAbundanceRaster))

    # First, we create the points of high percentile of growth on which we will fit the GAM - see documentation in previous cells for more
    # WARNING : To find the raster for the species, the species code here must be the ones used by the NFI.
    # see https://nfi.nfis.org/resources/general/3-TreeSpeciesList-Version4.5.pdf
    dictOfPointsSubsets = createPercentilesBiomassDatasetsFromNFIData(abundance_raster_path = './ReferencesAndData/NFI_Data/'+ str(fileNameSpeciesAbundanceRaster),
                                                                        age_raster_path = './ReferencesAndData/NFI_Data/NFI_MODIS250m_2011_kNN_Structure_Stand_Age_v1.tif',
                                                                        biomass_raster_path = './ReferencesAndData/NFI_Data/NFI_MODIS250m_2011_kNN_Structure_Biomass_TotalLiveAboveGround_v1.tif',
                                                                        speciesName = speciesName,
                                                                        study_landscape_shp_path = "./ReferencesAndData/SpatialBoundaries/ManawanTerritoryExtent.shp",
                                                                        canadian_ecozones_shp_path = "./ReferencesAndData/SpatialBoundaries/canadianEcozones.shp",
                                                                        age_window = 5,
                                                                        abundance_window = 5,
                                                                        percentile = 100,
                                                                        mask_path = None,
                                                                        printPlot = False)

    # Then, we create the GAMs and select the best oen (depending on the geographic area taken)
    peakHeightAndAgeDictionnary = getGamDiagnostics(dictOfPointsSubsets,
                                    speciesName,
                                    exportCsvOfSelectedCurve_Path = "./ReferencesAndData/Growth Curves Predictions/" + str(speciesName) + "_gam_predictions.csv",
                                    printPlot = True,
                                  dictionnaryOfSelectedCurves = "./ReferencesAndData/Growth Curves Predictions/dictOfSelectedCurvesSubsets.json")

    # Finally, we retrieve the peak height and peak 
    print(peakHeightAndAgeDictionnary)

    # We put it in the dictionnary of tolerances and targets
    SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode].update(peakHeightAndAgeDictionnary)

    print("\n\n")

# We save the dictionnary as updated
print(SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets)

with open('./ReferencesAndData/Functional Traits/SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets, f)

records.content:
The article from 

Tompalski et al. 2025 proposes stand-level yield curves (e.g. evolution of the biomass through the aging of a stand) for stands dominated by a particular tree species (27 species available). The curves have been created using different sources of remote sensing and aerial laser data. Furthermore, these curves are developed inside 150x150km tiles across Canada, with one curve per tile (as long as enough data for a given species was available in the tile).

We can use the data from the article, which is freely available in a very useful R package (see 

ptompalski/RSYC), in order to assess the estimates we have obtained with the GAMs.

Warning

Keep in mind that the biomass curves produced by Tompalski et al. 2025 are made with a different methodology than what we’re doing here. In particular, their curves represent the biomass of a stand dominated by a given species; but they don’t seem to remove the biomass from other species that will be inside the stand with the dominating species. Here, thanks to the NFI relative abundance raster, we do isolate the biomass for a given species. As such, species that can be dominant but which are still often not “very dominant” (not a lot of % relative biomass) will have their biomass mixed in with other species in the curves of Tompalski et al. 2025. This makes it that the curves of Tompalski et al. 2025 will often have higher biomass peaks than what is computed here for a given species; especially for species which display a low relative abondance accross most of their pixel because they are often companion species, like Acer rubrum.

Also, the curves of Tompalski et al. 2025 often display no senescence/decrease in biomass, because they focus on the stand level and not on the species in the stand. Meanwhile, or method based on the NFI data does track the senescence of given species inside a stand more easily, since we can isolate the biomass from the species of interest.

I’ve made a R script (that I have not saved, sadly) that retrieved the tiles from Tompalski et al. 2025 that intersect with the spatial extent of the subset of data used by the GAMs (see baove) for three species (Abies balsamea, Acer rubrum, and Picea mariana) from RSYC_tiles.gpkg that is available on 

ptompalski/RSYC, and then retrieved the maximum biomass observed accross all tiles (already extracted into a .csv file named species_max_biomass_TompalskiEtAl2025.csv using the R script extractingMaximalBiomassFromTompalskiEtAl2025.R; we had to use an R script R since 

ptompalski/RSYC is a R package).

Finally, the function displayed a bar plot showing the difference for each species between the biomass peak we got with the GAMs versus the average peak across all tiles intersecting the spatial extent used for the GAM in the curves of Tompalski et al. 2025. The black bar on the top of the Tompalski et al. bar shows the minimum and maximum observed across the model for all available tiles; it’s not the standard deviation.

Here are the plots resulting from this (which I have saved) :

As we can see, the maximum biomass observed in Tompalski et al. 2025 agrees quite well with the GAM for Abies balsamea  and Picea Mariana. However, the GAMs give a much lower estimates in our case for Acer rubrum. My own interpretation is that because the methodology of Tompalski et al. makes growth curves for “stands dominated by a given species”; as such, the biomass of their curves also contains the biomass of trees other than the dominant one. Here, we’re isolating the biomass of each species from the one of other species completely, thanks to the relative abundance raster of the NFI. Because Acer rubrum is most often a companion species with a relatively low dominance (even when it is dominant in a stand), I expect that this curves from Tompalski et al. are much higher because they tend to contain the biomass of other species, while ours focuses on Acer rubrum.

As such, I’ll keep going with what the GAMs have generated here.

records.content:
The approach used here has a lot of advantages (replicable, using open-source and peer-reviewed datasets that are easily available, can be adapted to future datasets of the same format, etc.). But it also have important limitations. In particular, two things have to be kept in mind :

We are using a statistical model to extrapolate, which means that the estimates that we get here are nothing but estimates.

Data from the NFI is on 250x250m cells, and is generated through k-NN association with sample plot data; what this means is that there is a lot of averaging going on. As a consequence, if you compare the estimate of biomass in tons/ha obtained here with sample plot data, you will tend to see much higher maximum biomass measure from the sample plot data, since there a lot more extreme values than in the NFI data.

The most important thing in the end is to make sure that the estimates of growth in ideal conditions we got here represent the differences between species quite well : whatever the values themselves, the ranking of species in terms of their growth capacity should be similar to their biological reality. It’s hard to consider that when we mix it with the fact that there is an important intra-specific variability that can mix things up; but overall, if you see one species that is supposed to be not very competitive achieving a lot of biomass compared to another one that is know to be very competitive or to achieve a lot of biomass, you might want to take a look at your curves again, and maybe select a different spatial extent or dataset.

Still, I consider this approach to be quite solid, especially after I contacted several researchers to get their opinion on it.

records.content:
If you find a better approach, know that the growth curves selected here are simply exported into .csv files (in the folder ReferencesAndData/Growth Curves Predictions/) which are then re-used in the subsequent notebooks for the calibration of PnET-Succession.

As such, you can implement you better approach by modifying this notebook, and export the curves your approach produces in a .csv file for each species (you can use the same names, e.g. Latin name_gam_predictions.csv). In that way, your approach will integrate nicely with the rest of the calibration, and you won’t have to change much things.

For the US, you might want to use this dataset instead of the NFI, which is quite similar : 

https://​data​.fs​.usda​.gov​/geodata​/rastergateway​/bigmap​/index​.php.

hierarchy.lvl2: Ranges of parameter values for the calibration

records.content:
We’re going to need bounds of parameter values during the calibration, so that we know when to stop increasing or decreasing a given parameter during a given calibration step.

Problem is, it’s hard to get different values of parameters for a given tree species, especially the rarer ones. Doubly so because almost every study that have used PnET-Succession so far (and which have published parameter values) don’t specify the primary source of these parameters in detail.

What I’m going to try to do here is to catalog different parameters values found in previous studies, but not per species; but rather for different characteristics (i.e. deciduous versus coniferous, level of shade tolerance or drought tolerance, etc.). By doing this, we only have to label a given tree species properly, even if it is rare (i.e. finding out if the species is supposed to be shade tolerant or not, deciduous or coniferous, etc.), and we can then have parameter bounds for it.

Note that some parameters (e.g. FolN) are affected both by the taxa of the species (coniferous versus deciduous) and their successional status (shade tolerant versus shade intolerant, or pioneer versus climax). However, I’ll only put them in one category of variation - the one I expect where the variation will be greatest.

## COMPUTING THE BOUNDS
# Now that have all of the values together, we are going to compute the bounds for each parameter.
# For that, we use some functions that will extend the bounds to deal with uncertainty when we have
# few values, and functions that will compute bounds with a linear model 

# WARNING : If you add new cells before this one or if you change the name of the notebook, you'll have
# to re-specify the new cell number and the the new notebook name, or the functions will not work.

from functionsForCalibration import *

# Reading the table with shade tolerance variation
shade_tolerance_parameters = parse_parameters_bounds_table("./4.Getting_the_empirical_data.ipynb", 26)
# Computing the bounds from it
bounds_shadeTolerance_Parameters = compute_all_bounds(shade_tolerance_parameters, expansion_factor=0.5, trend_min_n = 4, verbose=False)

# Reading the table for drought tolerance variantion
drought_tolerance_parameters = parse_parameters_bounds_table("./4.Getting_the_empirical_data.ipynb", 28)
# Computing the bounds from it
bounds_droughtTolerance_Parameters = compute_all_bounds(drought_tolerance_parameters, expansion_factor=0.5, trend_min_n = 4, verbose=False)

# Reading the table for waterlogging tolerance variantion
waterlogging_tolerance_parameters = parse_parameters_bounds_table("./4.Getting_the_empirical_data.ipynb", 29)
# Computing the bounds from it
bounds_waterloggingTolerance_Parameters = compute_all_bounds(waterlogging_tolerance_parameters, expansion_factor=0.5, trend_min_n = 4, verbose=False)

# Reading the table for coniferous versus angiosperms
coniferousVersusAngiosperms_parameters = parse_parameters_bounds_table("./4.Getting_the_empirical_data.ipynb", 27)
# Computing the bounds from it
bounds_coniferousVersusAngiosperms_Parameters = compute_all_bounds(coniferousVersusAngiosperms_parameters, expansion_factor=0.5, trend_min_n = 4, verbose=False)

# Reading the table for evergreen versus deciduous
evergreenVersusDeciduous_parameters = parse_parameters_bounds_table("./4.Getting_the_empirical_data.ipynb", 30)
# Computing the bounds from it
bounds_evergreenVersusDeciduous_Parameters = compute_all_bounds(evergreenVersusDeciduous_parameters, expansion_factor=0.5, trend_min_n = 4, verbose=False)

records.content:
Now that we have initial parameters for our species, we need to define the landscape in which the calibration will take place.

To adapt to the Python functions I’ve made for these Jupyter Notebooks (see 

here), simulations will take place on a single cell.

What we need is :

The climate file (containing all the climate variables need by PnET)

The ecoregion file and PnET ecoregion parameters (to decide which ones will be used during the calibration)

Initial communities files (will be created on the spot during the calibration depending on what we need)

hierarchy.lvl2: The climate file

hierarchy.lvl1: Notebook 5 - Getting the climate and soil data
hierarchy.lvl2: The climate file

records.content:
See 

Gustafson and Miranda (2023) for detailed information about what the climate file used in PnET simulations must contain.

Gustafson and Miranda (2023) recommends using a constant climate for calibrating (p. 69). Gustafson also recommends using an “ideal” climate at some points, but deciding what is an ideal climate for a given species is a difficult proposition. As we are going to compare the growth curves generated by PnET Succession with the growth curves estimated from data of the National Forest Inventory (NFI) of Canada (see 

​.​/5​.Other​_important​_data​_before​_starting​.ipynb), the idea will be rather to get long-term monthly averages (as recommended by 

Gustafson and Miranda (2023) p. 73) in the study landscape.

PnET Succession requires 5 climate variables to function :

Maximum Monthly temperature (°C)

Minimum Monthly temperature (°C)

Photosynthetically Active Radiation (umol/m2/s)

Sum of precipitations during the month (mm)

Mean monthly atmospheric CO2 concentration (ppm)

Making a climate file for PnET-Succession, or for the Climate Library of LANDIS-II ?

There are two ways of providing climate data to PnET-Succession :

Either with a climate file that is read directly by PnET-Succession (with the internal parsing code of PnET-Succession). See 

the user guide of PnET-Succession, section 6 to see what the file must look like.

With the dedicated “Climate library” of LANDIS-II. The climate library is made so that users can provide a single climate data file for several extensions of LANDIS-II. To use it with PnET-Succession, you must make a climate file (or even 2 if you want to make a separate file for the spinup climate data) according to the syntax highlighted in the 

user guide of the climate library. Then, you must make a “Climate config file” to indicate to the climate library where to find the climate files, and how to interpret them (there are several options indicated in the user guide; for example, you can use the average values of each months, etc. See the user guide for more). For an example of this climate config file, see 

ClimateConfigSimpleSims​.txt. Then, you just have to give the location of this config file to PnET-Succession through the parameter ClimateConfigFile in the main parameter file of PnET-Succession.

Here, we will be making a climate file to be used with the climate library of LANDIS-II. I think that this is the approach most recommended by the developers, and it’s also better on the long term so that you can re-use this scripts to make parameter files for your simulations with other LANDIS-II extensions.

records.content:
Our study landscape for this calibration (see 

0​.2​.Goals​_and​_assumptions​.ipynb) is located in Quebec, in the Mauricie region.

We will thus get climate data for this area. Since this is a large area, it will certainly cover several cells from the climate datasets we are going to use (see below). In addition, this area landscape two zones with a relatively different climate : warmer and more mixed forests in the south, and colder and more boreal forests in the north of the area.

In order to simplify things for this calibration, the climate stream we will use for our simulation will be the one of the climate cell closest to the center (centroid) of the study landscape. Therefore, the climate stream that we will generate here will not be averaged across space. But we will average it accross time (see below) in order to create “mild” and “ideal” conditions without extreme events, by averaging every day of the year accross a the historical period for which we have the data (i.e. every january 1st data for a given variable will the same, and will be the average of all january 1st in the original).

records.content: We’re going to make some climate data long in the past (1850-1950), just in case we need it for a spin-up procedure. But we’re mostly going to use historical data (1950-2014) as the climate used for our calibration, as it should be representative of the growth conditions that lead to the growth curves we’ve generated in Notebook 4. As such, in Notebook 6 and further, you will see that simulations start at 1950. After 2014 (when the climate data from the sources I’m using below stop), we’ll simply repeat the average values in the future.

records.content:
I did a lot of searching for a climate dataset that would be easy to access to make historical climate files for this calibration, but which would also be useful for “real” simulations once the calibration was done.

At first, I looked into the 

ESGF Metagrid, which is (to my understanding) an enormous decentralized catalog of climate data (data generated by Global Climate Models by different research time throughout the world). There is an enormous trove of data here, and it can be accessed quite easily using the 

intake-esgf Python package. However, one needs to search in this catalog to find datasets with historical data, the right variables, the right time step, etc. It was quite hard, and not always very stable; this is because the datasets are hosted on “nodes” run by several universities throughout the world, and these would sometime shut down or be unavailable for a time.

Then, a dataset was very recently published (at the time of writing this), in December 2025, by Ouranos - a Quebec-based research consortium on climate which regularly produces climate dataset for Canada or for all of North America. This dataset, which had a higher resolution that most others, seemed perfect for our use.

Here, I describe the dataset from Ouranos; 

see here for the article describing the dataset.

this pdf from the 

Zenodo webpage of the dataset for the variable list) :

tasmax => Daily or monthly

tasmin => Daily or monthly

rsds (downwelling shortwave radiation) => 1 hour or daily or monthly

pr (precipitations, liquid or solid !) => 1 hour or daily daily or monthly

The dataset also seem to contain all the variables needed for BFOLDS succession (wind speed and direction, relative humidity, etc.).

Resolution : 0.11 degrees, about 12 km resolution, timestep of 5 minutes inside the model.

Simulations are produced at 0.11° horizontal grid spacing over North America from 1950 to 2100.

Up to four different emissions scenarios are used for future climate simulations (2015–2100).

Therefore, historical data is for 1950-2015.

Here, we will use Monthly data, as we don’t need to go at a daily basis.
