hierarchy.lvl3: Climate data source for 🌡️ temperature, 🌧️ precipitation and ☀ solar radiation
hierarchy.lvl2: The climate file

records.content:
Explanation of the datasets according to the paper (

https://​www​.nature​.com​/articles​/s41597​-025​-06289-7) :

Simulations driven by GCMs from the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project 6 (CMIP6) cover years 1950–2014 for the historical period, while future climate simulations span 2015–2100. Future simulations follow several shared socioeconomic pathway (SSP) climate scenarios. SSP1-2.6 and SSP3-7.0 are required by CORDEX. Simulations following SSP2-4.5 are also run given interest in this scenario for climate adaptation. One simulation following the high-emissions SSP5-8.5 scenario has also been carried out.

All simulations seem to have been done with only one climate model, called CRCM5. CRCM5 is not a Global Climate Model (GCM); it is a Regional Climate Model (RCM). RCMs allow for a higher resolution and to account for regional specificities; but they require climate conditions on the bounds of the area that is simulated. These climate conditions are thus taken from GCM - which simulate the entire earth. So, CRCM5 only simulates Canada, but needs data from GCMs to generate results. As such, we say that the RCM is “driven by” a given GCM.

Although selecting only four models out of 56 may seem like a very small sample, most are not suitable for RCM application due to technical or compatibility constraints. Moreover, many of these models are interdependent (see16), which further limits the effective number of independent simulations to be selected. To elaborate further, models are selected based on several criteria, including data availability, model performance, climate sensitivity and compatibility with the CRCM5. The most important criterion is the availability of the data needed to drive the simulations. We require driving data for the historical simulation, which is an entry deck for CMIP6, and future simulations from the ScenarioMIP project. Data for SSP1-2.6, SSP2-4.5, and SSP3-7.0 must be available. As CRCM5 can handle only Gregorian or fixed 365-day calendars, models with a fixed 360-day calendar are excluded.

They also justify that these 4 models tend to have different climate sensibility (for a given augmentation of atmospheric CO2 in a simulation, the models tend to generate more or less increase in temperature) :

We also attempted to span a wide range of equilibrium climate sensitivity (ECS) values, which represent the amount of long-term warming resulting from a doubling of atmospheric CO2 concentrations. The ECS values for our selected driving GCMs include 5.64 K for CanESM5, 4.8K for CNRM-ESM2-1, 3.0K for MPI-ESM-1-2-LR and 2.54 K for NorESM2-MM.

Other criteria :

Additionally, more subjective selection criteria include storage space required for the driving data (related to model resolution), collaborations with model developers (CanESM5), and use of the model by other regional modeling communities such as EURO-CORDEX (CNRM-ESM2-1). The possibility of using several members favored the selection of MPI-ESM-1-2-LR.

Performance of the models :

Performance of the driving GCMs globally and over North America was also considered by comparing key driving variables to ERA5, including temperature, specific humidity, mean and variance of geopotential, sea surface temperature, and sea ice extent (e.g. Model Selection Dashboard. However, this has been used only for the selection of last GCM (NorESM2-MM).

Biases :

The paper identifies several biases, especially in Greenland, southern Canada and Alberta/Manitoba. Also precipitation biases with a east-west gradient (too much precipitation in the east, too much dryness in the west). These biases are calculated by comparing ERA5 versus CRCM5 drive by ERA5. Cold biases are also found by comparing ERA5 to CRCM5 driven by the 4 other models than ERA5. Biases in summer seem weaker. Strong precipitations biases in the oceanic zones ? Might be related to cyclone devellopment. But these are not so much present in Canada per se, more California and mexico.

CRCM5 driven by CanESM5 predicts the strongest warming, with CRCM5 driven NorESM2-MM predicting the weakest warming.

Although the high ECS of CanESM5 has been deemed unlikely (see 24), it remains useful as part of a narrative approach to examine potential outcomes in very warm scenarios for North America.

In the case of the 4 models given here, CNRM-ESM2-1 is the closest to the average equilibrium climate sensibility (ECS) of the 4 models (average is around 3.99K, MPI-ESM-1-2-LR has a value of 4.8K). MPI-ESM-1-2-LR is also a good choice.

records.content:
To simplify things during the calibration, we could choose only one stream of data from one of the driving GCMs. Criteria possible : the one with the most average equilibrium climate sensibility (ECS); the one that has the least bias / performs best on historical or verification data.

The main author/contact for the article, Dominique Paquin, recommanded that we use MPI-ESM-1-2-LR as a driver model, as it seems to be the one that produces the most reliable data according to her (although she precised that using several models is always best; which is what we will do in the simulations after calibrating PnET-Succession.

Why we are using a single climate model (instead of several together) here

Climate scientists often recommend to use the results from several climate models (GCMs) at once so as to explore the influence of the variability in results that exists between models, and the incertainty that results from it.

Here, I choose to use the results from a single climate model for several reasons :

The goal of this calibration is not to explore climate change predictions, but simply to calibrate the growth and dynamic of our tree species. For that, we will use historical data simulated by the model we chose (TaiESM1).

This calibration is already quite complex, and I’d like to keep it as simple as possible.

Using several models at once requires one of two things : either using a single climate file whose values are averaged along the values simulated by the different GCMs, which results in very, very “mild” climate conditions (since all extreme variations are not synchronized between models); or to use several climate files and create several PnET-Succession growth curves at each point (which makes things quite complicated).

However, once you have calibrated PnET Succession and will use it in your LANDIS-II simulations, it might be good to generate different climate files based on different climate models in order to create “climate replicates” for your LANDIS-II simulation that will allow you to generate an envellope of variability/uncertainty on your LANDIS-II results. To that end, you can use the scripts of this page to generate several climate files for different climate models available in ESGF (be warned that not all models have all of the variables you will need for all of the climate scenario you will want to explore). You can also use the functions from the Xclim python package that will allow you to select a smaller number of models that will represent your climate data properly (see 

here; you will need to create a large ensemble of climate data to use these functions).

# The code in this cell loads the functions needed to extract the climate data from the PAVIS database from Ouranos,
# using the CanDSC-M6 dataset
# We will use it to extract minimum and maximum monthly temp + total monthly precipitation for climate scenario SSP126
# The code in this cell will be re-used on other pages to generate the values for other climate scenarios
# Right now, it doesn't really matter since the first calibration step (monoculture in best conditions) will use
# historical data

# Importing the packages needed
# Siphon is used to connect to the data,
# xarray is used to manipulate the data objects
# The rest is for progress bars, utilities
# Clisops is used to subset the data based on a polygon
from functionsForCalibration import *
from siphon.catalog import TDSCatalog
import xarray as xr
from dask.diagnostics import ProgressBar
from IPython.display import display
from clisops.core import subset
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from tqdm import tqdm

# This function will help us define the URL to get the right dataset,
# corresponding to the right variable; the right SSP scenario; and the right model.
# This URL was made by browsing the THREDD server catalog of datasets, as was recommanded to me by email by the Canadian Centre for Climate Services (ECCC
# It points to the location of monthly maximum temperature data for CanDCS-M6, and climate scenario SSP126. The models are from CIMP6 from the IPCC.
def getURLDatasetHistoricalCRCM5CMIP6(GCM, replicate):
    url = ("https://pavics.ouranos.ca/twitcher/ows/proxy/thredds/dodsC/datasets/simulations/RCM-CMIP6/CORDEX/NAM-12/mon/NAM-12_" +
           str(GCM) +
           "_historical_" + 
           str(replicate) + 
           "_OURANOS_CRCM5_v1-r1_mon_195001-201412.ncml")
    return url

# We define the time period of the data we want and prepare the dataframe
# that we will export for PnET - that will ultimatily contain a monthly measure
# of Tmin, Tmax, Precpitations, CO2 and PAR for each months of each year
# We will go up to 2015, the historical data limit in CRCM5-CMIP6
print("Initializing data ranges and objects...")
startYear = 1950
endYear = 2014
years = np.repeat(np.arange(startYear, endYear+1), 12)
# Create months 1-12 for each year
months = np.tile(np.arange(1, 13), (endYear+1)-startYear)
# Create DataFrame
df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Year': years,
    'Month': months
})
numberOfMonths = len(df["Month"])

print("Opening the dataset...")
# We load the data for the driving GCM decided in the previous cells, with
# its first replicate name (taken from the THREDDS server of ouranos)
ds = xr.open_dataset(getURLDatasetHistoricalCRCM5CMIP6("MPI-ESM1-2-LR",
                                                      "r1i1p1f1"))

# This prints information about the dataset
# display(ds)

print("Subsetting to the study landscape...")
# Subset the dataset by polygon
# What we get in ds1 is an array of grid cells with a third dimension corresponding to time
ds1 = subset.subset_shape(
    ds, shape="./ReferencesAndData/SpatialBoundaries/ManawanTerritoryExtent.shp"
) 

print("Dimensions of the dataset : " + str(ds1.dims))

#################################################
# MAXIMUM MONTHLY TEMPERATURE
#################################################
# We select the variable data and months we need - all of them
# 0 is january of 1950. numberOfMonths is the number of months from 1950, defined
# at the beginning of the cell.
print("Converting Maximum monthly temperature...")
ds_months = ds1["tasmax"].isel(time=slice(0, numberOfMonths))
# With this command, we can get the monthly averages across all cells in one very quick step
listOfTmaxMonthly = kelvin_to_celsius(ds_months.mean(dim=['rlat', 'rlon']).values.tolist())
df["Tmax"] = listOfTmaxMonthly

#################################################
# MINIMUM MONTHLY TEMPERATURE
#################################################
print("Converting Minimum monthly temperature...")
# We select the variable data and months we need - all of them
ds_months = ds1["tasmin"].isel(time=slice(0, numberOfMonths))
# With this command, we can get the monthly averages across all cells in one very quick step
listOfTminMonthly = kelvin_to_celsius(ds_months.mean(dim=['rlat', 'rlon']).values.tolist())
# print(ds1)
df["Tmin"] = listOfTminMonthly

#################################################
# PRECIPITATIONS
#################################################
print("Converting monthly precipitations...")
# We select the variable data and months we need - all of them
ds_months = ds1["pr"].isel(time=slice(0, numberOfMonths))
# With this command, we can get the monthly averages across all cells in one very quick step
# Precipitations are relative to a surface, so we can average them without issues.
listOfPrecTotMonthly = ds_months.mean(dim=['rlat', 'rlon']).values.tolist()
# Conversion of preciptations
# In the CRCM5-CMIP6 dataset, the units of precipitations are kg m-2 s-1 (for a second, but averaged other a whole month).
# See the metadata of ds_month for a validation of this.
# In PnET-Succession, the unit that we need depends on how we feed the climate stream to PnET-Succession :
# - If we give the climate stream directly to PnET-Succssion with its internal library,
#   the units is sum of precipitations for the month in mm (see PnET-Succession user guide)
# - If we give the climate stream through the LANDIS-II climate library, we can give it as
#   daily or monthly sum, but it is in cm (see the user guide of the climate library, table 1.
# Here, we prepare a file for the climate library of LANDIS-II; as such, we go from instannaeous fluxes in kg m-2 s-1
# to a monthly sum in cm.
# As such, to convert, we need to adapt to the changing amounts of seconds in a month (depends on the number of days in the month)
# We prepare a list of days in the month. This is a bit complex, so we use a custom function to do the trick.
# See docstring of convert_monthly_precip_to_cm() for more.
listOfPrecTotMonthly = convert_monthly_precip_to_cm(listOfPrecTotMonthly, (1950, 1), (2014, 12))
df["SumOfPrecipitations"] = listOfPrecTotMonthly

#################################################
# PHOTOSYNTHETICALLY ACTIVATE RADIATION (PAR)
#################################################
print("Converting rsds daily to daytime...")
# First, we have to convert rsds (shortwave downwelling solar radiation)
# from daily averaged values from daytime averaged values (before we transform it into PAR)
# Even if we're looking at rsds at a monthly timestep (monthly averaged), this average is done
# for the 24 hours of each day of the month.
# However, PnET-Succession deals with the daytime hours for the solar radiation (precisely, it needs 
# "Mean monthly value of Photosynthetically Active Radiation during daylight hours"), meaning that we
# have to account for the daytime (sunrise to sunset) of each day of the month, then get the monthly
# daytime, and then make the conversion of solar radiation from daily to daytime.
# This function takes care of it, and creates a new climate variable in our dataset for that.
ds1 = convert_to_daylight_average(ds1, "rsds")
# We take the shortwave downwelling solar radiation, named rsds
ds_months = ds1["rsds_MonthlyDaytime"].isel(time=slice(0, numberOfMonths))
# With this command, we can get the monthly averages across all cells in one very quick step
listOfRadiationMonthly = ds_months.mean(dim=['rlat', 'rlon']).values.tolist()

print("Converting rsds_daytime to PAR...")
# Transforming downwelling shortwave radiation (rsds) in W/m2 to photosynthetically activate radiation PAR in umol.m2/s-1
# Based on the PnET User Guide's instructions
# Downwelling shortwave radiation (rsds which we have here) is often refered as global solar radiation.
# See https://library.wmo.int/viewer/68695/?offset=3#page=298&viewer=picture&o=search&n=0&q=shortwave . But other references exist.
# So, Downwelling shortave radiation is for wavelengths of 0.2–4.0 μm; but PAR is for 0.4–0.7 μm (lesser range).
# As such, to convert our Downwelling Shortwave Radiation in W/m2 to PAR in umol.m2/s-1, 
# we must multiply it by 2.02 as indicated in the user guide of PnET Succession. See the guide for more info.
listOfRadiationMonthly = [x * 2.02 for x in listOfRadiationMonthly]
df["PAR"] = listOfRadiationMonthly









#################################################
# EXPORT TO CSV
#################################################
# We output the dataframe to a .csv
# df.to_csv("./ReferencesAndData/dataFrameClimate_Manawan_Ouranos.csv", sep=',', index=False, encoding='utf-8')

# print(df)


# We rename the columns to match the names that the climate library of LANDIS-II expect
# PAR already has the right name
df = df.rename(columns={'tasmin': 'Tmin', 'tasmax': 'Tmax', 'SumOfPrecipitations':'precip'})

df["Ecoregion"] = "eco1"

# We print the result
print(df)

##################################
# Giving the dataframe the right columns
# for the LANDIS-II v8 climate library
##################################

# Create a unique identifier for each lat/lon pair
# mergedDataframe['lat_lon'] = mergedDataframe['lat'].astype(str) + '_' + mergedDataframe['lon'].astype(str)

# Melt the dataframe to convert variables into rows
melted_df = pd.melt(
    df, 
    id_vars=['Year', 'Month', "Ecoregion"],
    value_vars=['Tmin', 'Tmax', "PAR", "precip"],
    var_name='Variable',
    value_name='value'
)

# Pivot the table to get lat_lon as columns (which can then be used as ecoregions)
result_df = melted_df.pivot_table(
    index=['Year', 'Month', 'Variable'],
    columns='Ecoregion',
    values='value'
).reset_index()

# Convert the column index from MultiIndex to regular Index
result_df.columns.name = None
result_df = result_df.sort_values(by=['Variable', 'Year', 'Month'])

# We replace the name of the column that has the lat_lon pairing into an 
# ecoregion name (the one used in our PnET one cell scenario, eco1)
# result_df = result_df.rename(columns={result_df.columns[-1]: 'eco1'})
# print(result_df)

# We print as .csv
result_df.to_csv("./ReferencesAndData/Climate Data/dataFrameClimate_historicalMonthly_Ouranos.csv", sep=',', index=False, encoding='utf-8')
print(result_df)

records.content:
Now, we have to add the CO2. We’re going to reuse old code I’ve made for a previous climate data source (ESGF); the CO2 data comes from 

https://​zenodo​.org​/records​/5021361 .

Why are we adding CO2 data from a different dataset or model than the one used for the climate variables ?

From my small understanding and knowledge of Climate models (including Global Climate Models or GCMs, or Regional Climate Models like CRCM5 that we’re using here), the CO2 atmospheric concentration is most often treated in these models as a prescribed variable rather than an output variables. This means that the evolution of the values of the CO2 atmospheric concentration in these models is actually determined before the simulation, and is not influenced by the model during the simulation. The evolution is actually prescribed by the emission scenario chosen : the SSPs or RCPs scenarios.

Some rare climate models include feedbacks between human emissions of CO2 and atmospheric CO2 concentration, for example with vegetation feedbacks (see 

HadGEM2-ES. In these models, the CO2 atmospheric concentration is therefore another output of the model, and is influenced by the model and not completely fixed before the simulation (only human CO2 emissions are fixed by the emission scenario).

But since CO2 can still change seasonaly and locally (because of the effects of forests, etc.), and because CO2 is one of the variables driving photosynthesis in PnET-Succession, I thought it would be best to have a CO2 dataset that changes with time and with space, even if it’s from another model. Here, that’s what we’re using to add CO2 data to our climate file. It’s not super important for the calibration of PnET-Succession, and we could have just used yearly averages of CO2 atmospheric concentrations derived from historical data. But I was looking forward to future simulations that would use future data, and thought it best to do things right. Therefore, you can re-use this code to generate future CO2 concentration data for your study landscape after you’re done with this calibration.

# First, we download the CO2 data from https://zenodo.org/records/5021361 

from functionsForCalibration import *
import pandas as pd
import urllib.request
import subprocess
import os

# We use the zenodo_get library to download files from Zenodo very fast; other methods tend to be slow.
# See https://github.com/dvolgyes/zenodo_get . It is installed in the Docker image.
# WARNING : will take around 1.5GB of space for the data from 1950 to 2150 for a full climate scenario.

# We download the file with historical data
os.system("cd ReferencesAndData && zenodo_get 5021361 -g CO2_1deg_month_1850-2013.nc")

# We can also download data for future conditions, like this :
# os.system("cd ReferencesAndData && zenodo_get 5021361 -g CO2_SSP126_2015_2150.nc")





from functionsForCalibration import *

historical_ds = xr.open_dataset("./ReferencesAndData/CO2_1deg_month_1850-2013.nc")
# future_ds = xr.open_dataset("./ReferencesAndData/CO2_SSP126_2015_2150.nc")

# Shapefile to select where we take our values; if several climate cells are in the shapefile,
# then the CO2 values in all cells will be averaged (see function process_co2_data)
shapefile = "./ReferencesAndData/SpatialBoundaries/ManawanTerritoryExtent.shp"

# We load the dataframe
# We prepare the dataframe in a format that matches the older function I made
# When I was using monthly data and LANDIS-II v7.
# We load the file created previously
df_climate = pd.read_csv("./ReferencesAndData/Climate Data/dataFrameClimate_historicalMonthly_Ouranos.csv")
# Convert to year month day to string
df_climate['date_string'] = (df_climate['Year'].astype(str) + '-' + 
                     df_climate['Month'].astype(str))
# Get unique dates
uniqueDates = df_climate['date_string'].unique()
# Create the new dataframe
dfToFill = pd.DataFrame(columns=["Year", "Month", "Variable"])
# Put the dates in
dfToFill["date_string"] = uniqueDates
# extract the dates correctly
dfToFill['Year'] = dfToFill["date_string"].str.split('-').str[0].astype(int)
dfToFill['Month'] = dfToFill["date_string"].str.split('-').str[1].astype(int)
# Add variable name
dfToFill["Variable"] = ["CO2"] * len(dfToFill)
# Remove the date_string column
dfToFill.drop('date_string', axis=1, inplace=True)
df_climate.drop('date_string', axis=1, inplace=True)
# Now, the dataframe is ready to be filled with CO2 values with the old function !
print(dfToFill)

dfToFill = process_co2_data_monthly(historical_ds, shapefile, dfToFill)

# Display the updated dataframe
print(dfToFill)
print(dfToFill.head())
print(dfToFill.tail())



# Finally, we add this CO2 data to the previous data, and re-average it if needed

# First, we have to rename the CO2 column in the dataframe we just filled so that it corresponds to an ecoregion now
# (this is due to the difference in the climate library formats with LANDIS-II v8 and the old functions I was using before switching)
# The column name CO2_Concentration is created by the function process_co2_data
dfToFill = dfToFill.rename(columns={'CO2_Concentration': 'eco1'})

# We concatenate the dataframes
df_climate = pd.concat([df_climate, dfToFill], ignore_index=True)

# We check if needed
print(df_climate)

# We save everything
df_climate.to_csv("./ReferencesAndData/Climate Data/dataFrameClimate_historicalMonthly_Ouranos.csv", sep=',', index=False, encoding='utf-8')

# Now that everything is done, we can remove the CO2 files downloaded
os.remove("./ReferencesAndData/CO2_1deg_month_1850-2013.nc")
# os.remove("./ReferencesAndData/CO2_SSP126_2015_2150.nc")

records.content:
We will end up by making a “Spinup” climate file. This file will simply contain the averages of the measures between 1950 and 1980, and will then distribute these averages from the year 1700 to 1951. These will serve for spinup procedures of PnET-Succession to initialize the biomass of the cohorts in our simulations, where the model simulates the growth of the cohorts in the past in order to estimate their biomass at the beginning of the simulation.

import pandas as pd

# Read the climate data
df = pd.read_csv("./ReferencesAndData/Climate Data/dataFrameClimate_historicalMonthly_Ouranos.csv")

# Filter data for years 1950-1980
df_filtered = df[(df['Year'] >= 1950) & (df['Year'] <= 1980)]

# Calculate monthly averages for each variable
monthly_averages = df_filtered.groupby(['Month', 'Variable'])['eco1'].mean().reset_index()

# Create date range from 1700-01 to 1951-12
years = range(1800, 1952)
months = range(1, 13)
date_combinations = [(year, month) for year in years for month in months]

# Build new dataframe
new_data = []
for year, month in date_combinations:
    for _, row in monthly_averages[monthly_averages['Month'] == month].iterrows():
        new_data.append({
            'Year': year,
            'Month': month,
            'Variable': row['Variable'],
            'eco1': row['eco1']
        })

# Create and save new dataframe
df_new = pd.DataFrame(new_data)
df_new = df_new.sort_values(by=['Variable', 'Year', 'Month'], ascending=[True, True, True])
print(df_new)
df_new.to_csv("./ReferencesAndData/Climate Data/dataFrameClimate_SpinupMonthly_Ouranos.csv", index=False)

print(f"Created extended dataset with {len(df_new)} rows")
print(f"Date range: {df_new['Year'].min()}-{df_new['Year'].max()}")

records.content:
We finish by creating some averaged monthly inputs from our monthly measurements. We do this because 

Eric Gustafson recommends in his calibration tips to use averaged climate historical inputs for the calibration simulations. This is because averaged climate inputs tend to remove the extreme events from the climate data (e.g. very hot summers, very cold winters, droughts, etc.) and makes the climate “mild”, effectively removing the effect of extreme or even variable climate events on our calibration simulations. This is quite important.

The function average_climate_data_by_month() used here will create .csv files with a suffix _MonthlyAveraged that we can then use.

from functionsForCalibration import *

average_climate_data_by_month("./ReferencesAndData/Climate Data/dataFrameClimate_historicalMonthly_Ouranos.csv")
average_climate_data_by_month("./ReferencesAndData/Climate Data/dataFrameClimate_SpinupMonthly_Ouranos.csv")

records.content:
Eric Gustafson recommands in his calibration tips to use different types of pre-programmed soil textures according to the calibration step. For example, to use the SILO (Silty Loam/Silt Loam) soil from the default PnET Succession SaxtonAndRawls file (C:\Program Files\LANDIS-II-v7\extensions\Defaults\SaxtonAndRawlsParameters.txt) as a soil that retains water well for the first calibration step. This is because the first calibration step should use an “ideal soil” where water is well-retained so that lack of water (or watterlogging) does not influence growth at this calibration step.

We can use the SILO soil type easily by modifying the SoilType of eco1 in the PnET One Cell Scenario that we will use (see SimulationFiles/PnETGitHub_OneCellSim_v8/). So, nothing more to do here.

Other soil types will be used in other steps to simulate drought (with a soil type that doesn’t retain water well) or waterlogging (with a soil type to retains too much water).

However, if you’re looking for soil data for your simulations after the calibration, you can check 

this dataset for Canada.

records.content:
The goal of this first calibration phase, as recommended by 

Gustafson and Miranda (2023), is to calibrate the growth and mortality of a given tree species in “ideal” conditions. This means simulating a monoculture (no light competition with another species) on a mesic soil that retains water correctly (no drought, but no watterlogging either) and with a very mild, average historical climate (no extremes, especially in precipitations and temperatures). What I’ve attempted here is to automatize the advice and tips of 

Gustafson and Miranda (2023) which can be complex.

This first calibration phase is really the only one taking place in this current version of the notebook, even though we might do some adjustments based on the results of the competition between the species (see Notebook 7). I had planned to calibrate the drought/waterlogging and temperature parameters, but have found it to be a quite complex endeavor for which we didn’t have the time yet. See 

notebook about future improvements for indications of possible future improvements.

In this first step, we can isolate the effect of the many parameters and factor that influence tree growth, and focus on the few parameters that should (on a theoretical level) influence tree growth and mortality in ideal conditions :

FracFol

FolN

TOWood

TORoot

SLWmax

FracFol (pioneers species tend to have relatively high values)

FolN (pioneers species tend to have relatively high values)

FracBelowG

Some others (see code sections and functions bellow)

There can be confusion between “optimal conditions in the study area” and “optimal conditions for the species”. The calibration tips of 

Gustafson and Miranda (2023) recommands the later; but so as we have produced growth curves as close as the study area as possible (see notebook 4) to avoid confusing variants and genotypes, I’m going here with using “optimal conditions in the study area”. What that means is that we’re going to use precipitations and temperatures that are from the study area, but use their historical values that we will average to produce a “mild” climate with no extremes and very regular conditions (see Notebook 5). We will then make sure that the species match the peak of biomass (age and height of the peak), which we obtained for data near the study landscape (see Notebook 4) in these mild but local climate conditions.

To give you a broad idea of what we’re going to do here (which I finally started to understand after a lot of experimentation), we’re going to make sure that in the absence of as much reduction factor of photosynthesis as we can (fTemp for temperatures, fWater for drought/waterlogging, fAge for longevity, etc.), the productivity of species is enough so that it reaches the biomass peak found in the empirical data. We’re going to adjust this productivity so that not only do we match the peak height and timing (age), but we do so with the right amount of foliage/LAI (to make sure that species of different shade tolerance have the right amount of LAI; see Notebook 4 as to where we get these LAI targets), and by making sure that the beginning of the growth curve is acceptable. Finally, we calibrate the senescence using the methodology of Brian Sturtevant’s team.

All of this is a jungling act between many parameters, because changing one parameter (e.g. FolN for the overall productivity of the cohort) will often change different things in return (e.g. changing both the peak age and the peak height and the LAI). That why we will do it in different “substeps” where we try to focus on one aspect of the curve at the time.

I’ve composed the algorithms of each substeps myself (e.g. “First, change FolN until the peak height is right; then, change SLWMax to change the LAI; etc.”), and then used AI models to transform them into python functions (which are located in functionsForCalibration.py). Their outputs can change a bit and not be completly uniform, but I made sure that each functions describes precisely what is going on (i.e. what parameter is being changed, what is the target, etc.).

records.content:
TOWood and TORoot are used as kind of “garbage bins” to adjust the height of the biomass peak without changing the speed/age/timing of the peak too much by throwing away a part of the biomass of the cohort at each timestep. It’s really hard to have empirical/realistic estimates of the values of these two parameter for real trees, and to be able to juggle all of the other parameters without dealing with a lot of interactions between their effects; because TOWood and TORoot are quite predictable in their effect, their are used a lot to alter the biomass peak height. I’ve talked with Eric Gustafson, and he told me that it’s surely quite an inevitable part of the model (that we need to have some parameters that allow us to adjust the rest). I quite agree with him.

The algorithms can sometimes get stuck into the wrong places of the parameter space despite the checks I’ve implemented. This is because all the algorithms are quite simple, and usually change one or two parameters at a time until reaching a target. It worked quite well for me so far; but if you’re getting strange results or strange outputs, you should read the outputs to see what’s going on, and most likely adjust your initial parameter values (or the criterias/targets of the functions). One of the most common thing that happen is that increasing FracFol doesn’t increase the LAI of the cohort because we’ve put it in the situation where the cohort is starved for carbon; you can detect that by seeing that the wood biomass of the cohort is very low.

You can imagine that the algorithms trying to find the right combination of parameters for your targets is a bit like playing golf. Our goal is to reach a certain area of the parameter space, where the model gives us the behavior that we want. Moving the golf ball across the parameter space is done by changing the values of our parameters. But it’s like the golf course (the parameter space) contains hazards, holes, bunkers, water surfaces, or other things where our golf ball can get trapped.



Figure 1:The golf course as a metaphor for the PnET-Succession parameter space that we’ll be trying to traverse, filled with sand bunkers in which our ball can get trapped. While the algorithms used here try to avoid such traps as much as possible, there’s a good change that one of the algorithms will get stuck for at least one of your species. 

Picture by GeorgeVezza on wikipedia.

Once we’re in such traps, it can be difficult or even impossible to get out by simply pushing our ball around as we’ve been doing so far. The algorithms I’ve designed here (with a lot of trial and error) are made to avoid these situations; but I’m certain that such situations will arise, especially because the parameter space of PnET-Succession is very big and complex. Also, keep in mind that sometimes, there simply isn’t any “end” to our golf course, because the target we’re trying to reach (or the combination of targets) is just impossible for the model to do with the given constraints (i.e. bounds on the parameters values, or simply the functioning of the model). In these cases, the algorithms are designed to return either a warning allowing you to manually change the parameters or reconsider your targets; or to return the best parameters that it found (closest to what you wanted).

One of the recurring problems is at the beginning of the growth of the cohort; sometimes, it’s quite difficult to make a cohort grow fast enough to reach its targeted biomass peak age, without making it grow too much, and vice-versa. When this happens, the algorithms are stretching the models into strange locations of the parameter spaces where you will start to see things like “bumps” in the LAI of the cohorts near its peak, etc. This means that we’re pushing into parameters that make the model behave quite unrealistically. If you’re getting stuck into these kind of situations, it might be good to double check your growth curves (Notebook 4) to be certain of what you’re asking the model to do. You might also try to change the generic parameters - like MaintResp -, but be warned that they will affect all the other species. Improvements of the algorithms (by editing the function used in the substep that is failing) is also possible.

Be certain of your weather data

Be certain to have good weather data. Because biomass accumulation is ultimately limited by water, your precipitation inputs should not be much greater than is typical for your study area, especially for the results of your simulation to match your empirical data better.

In this notebook, I’m using averaged climate data from 1950-2014 (see Notebook 5) as the climate data stream. The values are averaged in order to provide a “mild” climate that represent ideal growth conditions for the species (as recommended by Eric Gustafson). We’re taking data from up to 1950 to represent growing conditions as similar as possible to those who gave rise to the growth data we have used in notebook 4.

Empirical growth curves are less important than the relative differences between species

As recommended by 

Gustafson and Miranda (2023), keep in mind that calibrating growth to strictly match empirical growth curves may cause competition behavior in the model that is unrealistic. As such, you may want to favor a shape that is somewhat consistent with other species that have the same growth strategy (e.g., pioneers v. shade-tolerants). This will be important for the next phase (calibrating competition outcomes).

Also keep in mind that the model equations may produce a curve shape that is somewhat different than your empirical curves (likely generated under specific conditions) and forcing the model to exactly match empirical curves may reduce its validity in landscape simulations. In particular, you should try to match the biomass accumulation where growth begins to plateau and produce relative rates of initial growth that reflect each species’ growth strategy (i.e., continuum of pioneer v. shade-tolerant). (

Gustafson and Miranda (2023)).

The effect of longevity/age is disabled for the first substeps

For the first calibration substeps, you will see that the growth curves never go down, but will reach a plateau and stop.

This is because the first calibration substeps remove the effect of the longevity parameters (by putting the longevity of each species at 999 years), in order to remove the effect of age on growth.

The effect of age will be calibrated in section 1.4 with the calibration of senescence.

Code cells will display useful outputs

Each code cell will print detailed output about how the simulation is going on (evolution of the targets, parameter changes, etc.), allowing you to detect if one of the step is getting “stuck”, and why. When the calibration is finished for a species, the code will display a graph showing you the growth of the species wood biomass, LAI, and so on.

hierarchy.lvl2: Sub-step 1.1 : Calibrating the peak

records.content:
This substep is divided into two further sub-sub-steps, in order to follow the advice of 

Gustafson and Miranda (2023).

Sub-sub-step 1.1.1 : we adjust TOWood/TORoot for large adjustments of the growth curve peak. The objective is to match a peak that is a bit above empirical data (around 5-10%) to account for the fact that we are simulating in ideal conditions, while empirical data is not in ideal conditions. If we cannot adjust the peak sufficiently without going beyond the empirical limits of TOWood and TORoot (from previously published studies, see Notebook 4), an error message will be displayed with some diagnostics to help you re-work the initial parameter values for the species (e.g. maybe lower FolN, etc.), because your species might be growing way too much or not enough. In a sense, this substep acts as a “sanity check” for the rest; if the growth of your species is wildly different than what’s expected, this step will help you detect it and diagnose it. This will prevent you from wasting time and getting the other algorithms stuck because your initial parameters were producing values that did not make sense.

Sub-sub-step 1.1.2 : we adjust the LAI via changes in FracFol (for large adjustments), SLWMax (for fine-tuning), and FrActWd (for its stability, if it is unstable). Then, we re-adjust TOWood and TORoot to re-adjust the biomass peak. If we go beyond the empirical limits of TOWood and TORoot (from previously published studies, see Notebook 4), we will adjust FolN instead. If we still cannot adjust the biomass peak without going beyond the empirical limits of FolN, then an error message will appear. Once the biomass peak is re-adjusted, the algorithm will check if the LAI has changed too much because of this, and will attempt to re-calibrate the LAI, and then re-calibrate the peak once again. There’s a limit of 3 iterations of this bigger loop to try to make the LAI and the biomass peak converge. If they don’t, an error message will display.

These two sub-sub-steps use the biomass peak height and LAI targets defined in Notebook 4.

Exceptions in bypassing parameter bounds/limits

Some species can have more important values of TOWood and TORoot, bypassing previously published values. This is the case for the Red Maple (Acer rubrum), which is sometimes considered to have an important self-thinning at the tree scale and at the cohort scale according to discussions with Eric Gustafson. Sometimes, this increase in TOWood and TORoot is needed to make sure the species doesn’t grow too much despite correct initial parameters.

## Calibration sub-sub-phase 1.1.1 : We change TORoot and TOWood to make large adjustments to
# the biomass peak, while keeping them in bounds. If we can't make larget adjusments to the biomass
# peak enough while staying in the bounds, we run diagnostics and display information to the user.

from functionsForCalibration import *
import json

with open('./SpeciesParametersSets/speciesNamesDictionnary.json', 'r') as file: speciesDict = json.load(file)

with open('./ReferencesAndData/Functional Traits/SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets.json', 'r') as file: SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets = json.load(file)

resultDict = {}

# Modifying the FolN of some species who don't grow enough or grow too much with their
# initial parameters, which make it that they don't passe the first diagnostic test.
pnetSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json'))
# ABIE.BAL : Grows a bit too much, reducing FolN a little
pnetSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"]["ABIE.BAL"]["FolN"] = "1.1"
# ACER.SAH : Doesn't grow enough, increasing FolN a little
pnetSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"]["ACER.SAH"]["FolN"] = "2.6"
# ACER.SAH : LAI really isn't right. Increasing FracFol a little
pnetSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"]["ACER.SAH"]["FracFol"] = "0.023"
# LARI.LAR : Grows too much, reducing FolN a litle
# Not too much ! Because it's deciduous, it still has to renew its leaves every year
pnetSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"]["LARI.LAR"]["FolN"] = "1.5"
# pnetSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"]["LARI.LAR"]["FracFol"] = "0.022"
# Current SLWmax of LARI.LAR is really wrong compared to previous studies and LMA in TRY. Changing.
pnetSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"]["LARI.LAR"]["SLWmax"] = "93.58"

for speciesCode in speciesDict:

    if SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Evergreen"] == "Y":
        functionalType = "evergreen"
    else:
        functionalType = "deciduous"
        
    resultDict[speciesCode] = calibrate_phase1_1(
        species             = speciesCode,
        functional_type     = functionalType,
        target_peak_biomass = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Peak_Biomass"],      # g/m² — from NFI data
        LAI_min             = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Maximum LAI target"] - 1, # Arbitrary, based on my experience with the variation of the LAI
        LAI_target          = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Maximum LAI target"],
        LAI_max             = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Maximum LAI target"] + 1, # Arbitrary, based on my experience with the variation of the LAI
        dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters = json.load(
            open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json')),
        dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters = pnetSpeciesParameters,
        dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters = json.load(
            open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/InitialGenericParameters.json')),
        DictOfBounds        = json.load(open("./SpeciesParametersSets/SpeciesParameterBounds.json"))
    )

# Re-writing parameter dicts
dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json'))
dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters = pnetSpeciesParameters
dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/InitialGenericParameters.json'))

for species in resultDict:
    if resultDict[species] is None:
        raise ValueError("One of the initial diagnostics for one of your species (" + str(species) + ") has failed. Check the outputs of the cell to find out why, and modify the parameters until you can reach the right biomass peak with realistic values of TOWood and TORoot.")
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["TOWood"] = resultDict[species]["TOWood"]
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["TORoot"] = resultDict[species]["TORoot"]

if os.path.exists("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.1"):
    shutil.rmtree("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.1")
os.mkdir("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.1")

with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.1/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.1/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.1/InitialGenericParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters, f)

























## Calibration sub-sub-phase 1.1.2 : We calibrate and stabilize the LAI
# of the cohort to be within a range around the maximum LAI proposed by Gustafon
# in the user guide, by tweaking FracFol, SLWMax and FrActWood.

from functionsForCalibration import *
import json

with open('./SpeciesParametersSets/speciesNamesDictionnary.json', 'r') as file: speciesDict = json.load(file)

with open('./ReferencesAndData/Functional Traits/SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets.json', 'r') as file: SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets = json.load(file)

resultDict = {}

for speciesCode in speciesDict:

    print("#" * 80)
    print(f"CALIBRATING THE LAI OF {speciesCode}")
    print("#" * 80)
    
    resultDict[speciesCode] = calibrate_LAI_subphase_1_1_2(
        speciesCode,
        target_LAI_min=SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Maximum LAI target"] - 1,# Arbitrary, based on my experience with the variation of the LAI
        target_LAI_max=SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Maximum LAI target"],
        target_peak_biomass = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Peak_Biomass"],
        DictOfBounds=json.load(open("./SpeciesParametersSets/SpeciesParameterBounds.json")),
        initial_core_params_path="./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.1/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json",
        initial_pnet_species_params_path="./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.1/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json",
        initial_generic_params_path="./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.1/InitialGenericParameters.json",
        duration=300,
        climate="mild",
        soil="SILO",
        n_bisection_iterations=20,
        verbose=True
    )

# Re-writing parameter dicts
dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.1/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json'))
dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.1/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json'))
dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.1/InitialGenericParameters.json'))

for species in resultDict:
    # dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["FracFol"] = resultDict[species]["final_params"]["FracFol"]
    # dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["SLWmax"] = resultDict[species]["final_params"]["SLWmax"]
    # dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["FrActWd"] = resultDict[species]["final_params"]["FrActWd"]
    # dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["TOWood"] = resultDict[species]["final_params"]["TOWood"]
    # dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["TORoot"] = resultDict[species]["final_params"]["TORoot"]
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species] = resultDict[species]["updated_pnet_species_params"]["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]

if os.path.exists("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.2"):
    shutil.rmtree("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.2")
os.mkdir("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.2")

with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.2/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.2/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.2/InitialGenericParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters, f)

hierarchy.lvl2: Sub-step 1.2 : Calibrating the biomass peak age

hierarchy.lvl1: Notebook 6 - Calibration of species in optimal conditions (monoculture + mesic soil + average and unchanging climate)
hierarchy.lvl2: Sub-step 1.2 : Calibrating the biomass peak age

records.content:
In this sub-step, we focus on calibrating the age at which the biomass peak of the cohort happens. This has a large influence on the beginning of the growth curve, which in turn will influence the competition between our species a lot.

The biomass peak age is derived from the empirical growth curves in Notebook 4.

This sub-step was the most complicated for which to create an algorithm, because of two reasons :

We are trying to calibrate the peak age, but still keep the right peak height (amount of biomass) and the right LAI. But altering one often alters the others, meaning with have to do regular corrections to the peak height and the LAI as we keep exploring the parameter space to get the right peak age.

It’s more difficult to move the peak age than it is to move the peak height and the LAI. There are four parameters we act on for this : MaxFracFol and FracFolShape together at once; FrActWd; and FolN. MaxFracFol/FracFolShape and FrActWd have quite complex effects, especially FrActWd; it’s quite easy to make the algorithm gets stuck because we made the cohort starved from carbon, and we can’t adjust its biomass peak height or LAI anymore.

In the end, the algorithm is in three phases :

First, we try to find a combo of MaxFracFol and FracFolShape that 1. will get us closer to the peak timing and 2. will avoid making our cohort “fragile”, meaning that it might create very irregular and strange dynamics of LAI because of how MaxFracFol works. This is done by increasing FracFolShape very quickly as MaxFracFol increases, as FracFolShape will reduce the effect of MaxFracFol to conditions where light is plentiful only as we increase it.

Secondly, the algorithm will adjust FrActWd to change the peak timing; but every two iterations of adjustments, we re-adjust the peak height (with TOWood/TORoot) and the LAI (with FracFol) so that they match what we had at the start. This is so that we don’t put ourselves in a problematic situation where the LAI and the peak height become so absurdly large (or small) that our cohort simply cannot grow correctly anymore, which then messes up the algorithm as it tries to keep adjusting FrAcWd. By constantly adjusting, we might take a bit more time, but we’re coming to it quite well in the end.

The third and final section is optional. We come to it only if we were not able to get the right peak age alongside the right peak biomass and LAI without reaching the upper or lower empirical bounds of FrActWd in the previous phase. In this last phase, we will change FolN to increase/decrease the overall productivity of the cohort, still adjusting the biomass peak with TOWood/TOFol and the LAI with FracFol regularly.

If the algorithm succeeds in having a peak age, peak biomass and LAI all in range of tolerance, everything’s great ! If not (it happens), the algorithm will return the “best” parameter set it has found. Sometimes, especially if your peak age is too low, the algorithm will not be able to reach it. In this kind of situations, it might be better to embrace something that is close to your empirical data, even if it’s not at your empirical data.

There are several arbitrary values here : the amount by which we do increments on the different parameters, how fast FracFolShape changes with MaxFracFol, etc. All of these I’ve found through trial and error, especially as I was trying to calibrate the red maple - which according to the NFI data has a quite a quick peak time at 50 years, requiring a lot of adjustment.

If the algorithm doesn’t work for your species, you might have to use the advice above to find the right parameter set manually, by exploring the parameter space by yourself.

About the tips of Eric Gustafson for the wood biomass at age 14

In the 

User Guide of PnET-Succession, Eric Gustafson recommands (by his own experience with the model) to make sure that the cohorts achieve a certain amount of wood biomass by their age 14, in order to distinguish the species in terms of their shade tolerance, to make sure that the more intolerant ones grow faster than the others :

Shade Tolerance Category

Tolerant

Somewhat-tolerant

Intermediate

Somewhat intolerant

Intolerant

Wood biomass at age 14 (g/m2)

235

360

440

560

670

However, I was never able to reach these values. My species tended to grow way too slow for this. More generally, my biomass targets (biomass peak from the growth curves, see Notebook 4) were much lower than previously published studies. My current interpretation is this : Eric (and other researchers in the US) tended to use sample plot data or growth curves from the Forest Vegetation Simulator (which is based on sample plot data) to make the growth curves that served as references for this calibration step. However, I use the NFI data, which reports much lower values of biomass (in g/m2) than what sample plot data or the FVS give. It’s possible that it’s because the sample plot and FVS curves contain a lot of “extremes” because of the variability inherant to the sample plot data, while the NFI data is much more “averaged”, but most likely much closer to the truth too.

There’s also the question of transforming the estimates of biomass from sample plots from biomass (g) to biomass per surface (g/m2). There are always a lot of different hypothesis contained in this translation. The NFI data is already in g/m2, but sample plots and FVS curves often require to use hypothesis/assumptions to translate to biomass per surface. Again, I tend to think that the NFI data is closer to the mark, especially because it’s closer to what LANDIS-II does (LANDIS-II only does biomass per surface).

About the effect of MaxFracFol/FracFolShape and MaxFolN/FolNShape

These two couples of parameters have the goal of giving a boost of productivity/growth at the beginning of an age-cohort’s life. They do so by increasing FracFol or FolN, based on the following equations (which are the same for both couples of parameters) :

AdjFracFol = FracFol + ((MaxFracFol – FracFol) * (fRad^{FracFolShape}))

AdjFolN = FolN + ((MaxFolN – FolN) * (fRad^{FolNShape}))

As such, the “beginning of the cohort’s life” is actually define by the change in the value of fRad (the reduction factor due to the availability of light for the cohort). Taking AdjFracFol as example), when fRad is high (near 1, so no shade/open conditions), AdjFracFol = MaxFracFol. As fRad decreases (shade increases), AdjFracFol gets closer to FracFol. FracFolShape serves to modulate the speed at which the decrease (going from MaxFracFol towards FracFol) goes; the higher FracFolShape, the faster the decrease. So, increasing FracFolShape makes the window of the beginning of the life of the cohort where FracFol will be “boosted” narrower. Same equation and logic for MaxFolN/FolNShape.

These parameters are currently essential to calibrate the growth curve of the species, but they come with two big caveats :

Their effect might confusing because fRad really doesn’t behave in the way that one might expect depending on the situation. For example, with a single age cohort in a cell (like what we are simulating here), the average fRad doesn’t change very much (because the single cohort doesn’t do enough self-shading on itself). This makes so that the variation of AdjFracFol and AdjFolN will be minimal, despite the values of FracFolShape and FolNShape you will use.

If you calibrate the growth curve of your species using these parameters (and you really need to, because it’s very difficult to calibrate the initial growth of the species without them), you’re making sure that what you’re doing is going to act very differently as soon as you put your species in different conditions of shade. For example, this effect will completely disappear if your species implant in a pretty shaded pixel, or be too strong if it comes in completely open conditions, etc.

It will be good if the behavior of these parameters might be updated in the future, or if different parameters could be related to something more predictable and stable than fRad (for example, the age of the cohort directly); but that will be fore new versions of PnET-Succession. For now, I’m going with this. Just keep these limitations in mind, especially the fact that tree species that depend a lot on these parameters for their initial growth will be very suppressed if they grow in conditions with high shade.

This is the step most likely to fail

This calibration step is the most delicate, because we’re basically asking the model to grow a species at a certain speed, but also to reach a certain growth peak.

Some species will not manage this in the model, for different reasons : the climate that you have given is not good enough for them, or “too good”; the peak age and peak biomass are too big or too small relative to each other; and so on.

If this step fails, try to understand what’s going on, and check your growth curves. You can also relax the tolerance of the algorithm via additional parameters (see functionsForCalibration.py.

from functionsForCalibration import *
import json

with open('./SpeciesParametersSets/speciesNamesDictionnary.json', 'r') as file: speciesDict = json.load(file)

with open('./ReferencesAndData/Functional Traits/SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets.json', 'r') as file: SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets = json.load(file)

resultDict = {}

for speciesCode in speciesDict:

    resultDict[speciesCode] = calibrate_subphase_1_2(
        species = speciesCode,
        target_peak_time = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Peak_age"],   # years (from NFI data)
        target_peak_biomass = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Peak_Biomass"],
        target_LAI = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Maximum LAI target"],
        DictOfBounds = json.load(open("./SpeciesParametersSets/SpeciesParameterBounds.json")),
        path_core = './SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.2/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json',
        path_pnet = './SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.2/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json',
        path_generic = './SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.2/InitialGenericParameters.json',
        duration = 300,
        climate = "mild",
        soil = "SILO")

# Re-writing parameter dicts
dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.2/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json'))
dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.2/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json'))
dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.2/InitialGenericParameters.json'))

for species in resultDict:
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["MaxFracFol"] = resultDict[species]["MaxFracFol"]
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["FracFolShape"] = resultDict[species]["FracFolShape"]
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["FrActWd"] = resultDict[species]["FrActWd"]
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["FracFol"] = resultDict[species]["FracFol"]
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["TOWood"] = resultDict[species]["TOWood"]
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["TORoot"] = resultDict[species]["TORoot"]
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["FolN"] = resultDict[species]["FolN"]

if os.path.exists("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.2"):
    shutil.rmtree("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.2")
os.mkdir("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.2")

with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.2/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.2/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.2/InitialGenericParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters, f)

hierarchy.lvl2: Sub-phase 1.3 (optional) : Accelerating early growth for species that grow too slowly

hierarchy.lvl1: Notebook 6 - Calibration of species in optimal conditions (monoculture + mesic soil + average and unchanging climate)
hierarchy.lvl2: Sub-phase 1.3 (optional) : Accelerating early growth for species that grow too slowly

records.content:
By calibrating the peak timing (sub-phase 2), it happens that some species do indeed get the right timing; but that they spent the first decades (sometimes up to 50 years) of their lives almost not growing at all.

This seems to be the result of an overall lack of productivity for these species in their early years which translate to a low LAI, and vice versa. This lack of productivity/foliage can be caused by different things at once, like the species having a low productivity to begin with (low FolN), a combination of parameters that affects the LAI which make it difficult for the species to accumulate it in its early stages (FracFol, SLWMax, etc.), temperatures, etc.

The problem is that if we try to change these parameters, we also affect the rest of the growth of the species; especially its biomass peak timing and peak height.

I’ve tweaked a lot with different parameters to try to find something that would allow us to accelerate the early growth, while not moving the rest of the growth curve too much. It’s quite difficult, especially if we need to really increase the early growth of species. The best parameters I’ve found are MaxFracFol/FracFolShape ; and MaxFolN/FolNShape. These are made to affect only the early growth; MaxFracFol and MaxFolN increase FracFol and FolN respectively, while FracFolShape and FolNShape reduce the effect of MaxFracFol and MaxFolN as the canopy closes (as fRad diminishes). These parameters have been added to the PnET-Succession code by Eric Gustafson for this exact goal (influencing the early growth but not the rest of the curve as much as possible). So here, we increase these parameters to make the early growth faster; and because we will change the rest of the curve anyway, we correct the LAI with SLWmax, we correct the peak timing with FolN, and then we correct the peak height with TOWood and TORoot again. It’s really a game of jungling at this point. Hopefully, this is the last complex step.

There remains the question of the target of early growth; how much do we want to increase the early growth of the species ? When do we stop ? I pondered about using another target, that we would call the “Biomass reached at 50% of the peak timing”. We could extract this value from the NFI data and GAMs in notebook 4. But after trying to deal with this, I realize that for the Black spruce (PICE.MAR, which had a slower growth than necessary and which was the motivation for this step) demanded too much adjustments to reach the right value of its biomass reached at 50% of the peak timing (as derived from NFI data). The model simply wouldn’t go there without creating other big issues and pushing the parameters to extra high values. I’ve got some hypothesis as to why this might be; it could be issues with the NFI data which overestimate early growth, or the fact that the parameters in the current PnET-Succession equation are too static and should change with the age of the cohort (i.e. the allocation of photosynthesis to the different part of the trees, or its turnover of wood and leaves, etc., are not the same when the tree is young versus when it gets old).

So, I went with a simple rule that worked well in the case of the Black spruce : the biomass reached at 50% of the peak timing should be at least 30% of the biomass peak height. That’s what this calibration step tries to ensure. For the rest, I leave my trust in the hands of the PnET model.

This step is simpler than others and might get stuck

The calibration algorithm used here is a bit simpler than the others because there are more parameters, and because I was running out of time. It might get stuck and overshoot its targets at some points. Be careful with it !

This step will be skipped for most species

If the early growth of the species is already good enough for a given species, this step will simply be skipped.

from functionsForCalibration import *
import json

with open('./SpeciesParametersSets/speciesNamesDictionnary.json', 'r') as file: speciesDict = json.load(file)

with open('./ReferencesAndData/Functional Traits/SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets.json', 'r') as file: SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets = json.load(file)

resultDict = {}

for speciesCode in speciesDict:

    # Using a peak timing tolerance of 7 years seems OK to me; it's arbitrary, and you can change it if needed.
    # If the tolerance is to low, this algorithm might have trouble reaching it though.
    resultDict[speciesCode] = calibrate_subphase_1_3(
        species = speciesCode,
        target_peak_time = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Peak_age"],   # years (from NFI data)
        target_peak_biomass = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Peak_Biomass"],
        peak_time_tolerance = 7,
        target_LAI = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Maximum LAI target"],
        dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.2/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json')),
        dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.2/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json')),
        dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.2/InitialGenericParameters.json')),
        DictOfBounds = json.load(open("./SpeciesParametersSets/SpeciesParameterBounds.json")),
        duration = 300,
        climate = "mild",
        soil = "SILO")

# Re-writing parameter dicts
dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.2/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json'))
dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.2/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json'))
dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.2/InitialGenericParameters.json'))

# Detect if MaxFolN/FolNShape have been used for one of the species
# If that's the case, we will provide values that disable their effect for other species who didn't need it,
# as all species need to have the parameter
MaxFolN_FolNShape_enabled = False
for species in resultDict:
    if "MaxFolN" in resultDict[species].keys() or "FolNShape" in resultDict[species].keys():
        MaxFolN_FolNShape_enabled = True
        break

if MaxFolN_FolNShape_enabled:
    for species in resultDict:
        if "MaxFolN" not in resultDict[species].keys() or "FolNShape" not in resultDict[species].keys() or resultDict[species]["MaxFolN"] == "Parameter is not in dictionnary" or resultDict[species]["FolNShape"] == "Parameter is not in dictionnary":
            resultDict[species]["MaxFolN"] = dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["FolN"]
            resultDict[species]["FolNShape"] = 1

for species in resultDict:
    for parameter in ["MaxFracFol", "FracFolShape", "FracFol", "MaxFolN", "FolNShape", "FolN", "TOWood", "TORoot"]:
        # if parameter in resultDict[species].keys() and resultDict[species][parameter] != "Parameter is not in dictionnary":
        if parameter in resultDict[species].keys():
            dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species][parameter] = resultDict[species][parameter]

if os.path.exists("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.3"):
    shutil.rmtree("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.3")
os.mkdir("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.3")

with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.3/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.3/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.3/InitialGenericParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters, f)

records.content:
Focusing on :

PsnAgeRed

Longevity (LANDIS-II Core species parameter; is outside of PnET Succession)

The last phase of the curve to calibrate is the senescence phase, where the tree cohort grows older. At it grows, its maintenance cost (in carbon) will grow; but its photosynthesis will be reduced through a reduction factor, and will completely stop as the cohort reaches the “longevity” core species parameter for its species. This is how tree cohort die of old age in PnET-Succession : by becoming progressively unable to sustain their maintenance costs through photosynthesis.

As such, there are two targets that are being calibrated here : 1) the age where photosynthesis will start declining because of age, and 2) the age where the photosynthesis reduction factor will become so low that the cohort will die by lack of ressources. These two targets are shown on the following figure : This is a comment; I had to find a way to resize the image in Jupyter lab.
That makes for a snippet a code that is more complex than I'd want. See https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/1885. 

 



The first target (initiation of decline) is defined in PnET with the parameter PsnAgeRed, which controls when the photosynthesis reduction factor fAge starts declining, and how fast.

The second target (age of cohort death) is influenced by the start of the decline (first target), but also  with the “longevity” parameter given in the “Core” species parameter file (e.g. species.txt in /SimulationFiles/\PnETGitHub_OneCellSim_v8). When the age of the cohort reaches the “longevity” parameter, the photosynthesis reduction factor fAge will reach 0 (no more photosynthesis takes place). Of course, the cohort will most likely die before it reaches 0 because its photosynthesis will have been too low for too much time, which will have depleted its resources before fAge reaches 0. As such, we will try to input a “longevity” parameter value in the species parameter file that will kill the cohort at the age we want (e.g. that correspond to empirical data) in interaction with your first target. Note again that the cohort will very likely die before the longevity parameter.

As to where we can get estimates of these two targets (initiation of decline and age of cohort death) for our species, this gets a bit complex. I used (see Notebook 4) the methodology of Brian Sturtevant’s team, which define the initiation of decline as the “Typical longevity” of a tree species as published by 

Loehle (1988), and the age of death of the “Maximal longevity” available in the same study. However, other sources are possible; see Notebook 4 for more.

from functionsForCalibration import *
import json

with open('./SpeciesParametersSets/speciesNamesDictionnary.json', 'r') as file: speciesDict = json.load(file)

with open('./ReferencesAndData/Functional Traits/SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets.json', 'r') as file: SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets = json.load(file)

resultDict = {}

for speciesCode in speciesDict:

    resultDict[speciesCode] = calibrate_senescence(
        species = speciesCode,
        typical_mortality = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Longevity"], # TYPMORT from Loehle
        maximum_mortality = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Maximum longevity"], # MAXMORT from Loehle
        target_peak_biomass = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Peak_Biomass"],
        DictOfBounds = json.load(open("./SpeciesParametersSets/SpeciesParameterBounds.json")),
        dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.3/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json')),
        dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.3/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json')),
        dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.3/InitialGenericParameters.json')),
        tolerance=3,
        max_iterations=50,
        simulation_duration=500,
        climate="mild",
        soil="SILO")


# Re-writing parameter dicts
dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.3/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json'))
dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.3/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json'))
dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.3/InitialGenericParameters.json'))

for species in resultDict:
    for parameter in ["PsnAgeRed", "TOWood", "TORoot"]:
        dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species][parameter] = resultDict[species][parameter]
    dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters[species]["Longevity"] = str(resultDict[species]["Longevity"])
if "PsnAgeRed" in dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters.keys(): del dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters["PsnAgeRed"]

if os.path.exists("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.4"):
    shutil.rmtree("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.4")
os.mkdir("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.4")

with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.4/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.4/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.4/InitialGenericParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters, f)

from functionsForCalibration import *
import json

with open('./SpeciesParametersSets/speciesNamesDictionnary.json', 'r') as file: speciesDict = json.load(file)

with open('./ReferencesAndData/Functional Traits/SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets.json', 'r') as file: SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets = json.load(file)

listOfGrowthPnETCheckups = list()
listOfGrowthCurvesGAMS = list()

# For each species, we run some simple simulations with a) the initial set of parameters (uncalibrated) and b) more recent set so far
# We save the resulting growth curves into .csv files
# Then, we plot these growth curves next to each other.
# We will also plot the growth curves that came from the NFI data.
for speciesCode in speciesDict:

    calibrationSimulationMonoculturemanawan(duration = 300,
                                            climate = "mild",
                                            soil = "SILO",
                                            speciesToSimulate = speciesCode,
                                            dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json')),
                                            dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json')),
                                            dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/InitialGenericParameters.json')),
                                            plotResults = False,
                                           saveGrowthCurvePath = "./SpeciesParametersSets/Checkup_Step1/" + str(speciesCode) + "_Growth_PnET_initial.csv")
    
    calibrationSimulationMonoculturemanawan(duration = 300,
                                            climate = "mild",
                                            soil = "SILO",
                                            speciesToSimulate = speciesCode,
                                            dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.4/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json')),
                                            dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.4/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json')),
                                            dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.4/InitialGenericParameters.json')),
                                            plotResults = False,
                                           saveGrowthCurvePath = "./SpeciesParametersSets/Checkup_Step1/" + str(speciesCode) + "_Growth_PnET.csv")

    plot_growth_curves("./ReferencesAndData/Growth Curves Predictions/" + str(speciesDict[speciesCode]) + "_gam_predictions.csv",
                       "./SpeciesParametersSets/Checkup_Step1/" + str(speciesCode) + "_Growth_PnET.csv",
                      speciesCode,
                      "./SpeciesParametersSets/Checkup_Step1/" + str(speciesCode) + "_Growth_PnET_initial.csv")

    # We put the path of the NFI curves and the final growth curves in lists for the last plot where we will put all of the curves.
    listOfGrowthCurvesGAMS.append("./ReferencesAndData/Growth Curves Predictions/" + str(speciesDict[speciesCode]) + "_gam_predictions.csv")
    listOfGrowthPnETCheckups.append("./SpeciesParametersSets/Checkup_Step1/" + str(speciesCode) + "_Growth_PnET.csv")

# We then use another custom function to plot all of the growth curves of the tree species next to each other.
plot_multispecies_growth_curves(
    listOfGrowthCurvesGAMS,
    listOfGrowthPnETCheckups,
    list(speciesDict.keys())

)
